In the same source, with its chain to Safwan ibn Yahya, who said: I said to Imam al-Rida (عليه السلام): "We used to ask you before Allah granted you Abu Ja‘far [Imam al-Jawad], and you used to say: ‘Allah will grant me a boy.’ Now Allah has granted him to you, so our eyes are comforted. May Allah never show us your [final] day—but if something were to happen, to whom [should we turn]?" He pointed with his hand to Abu Ja‘far (عليه السلام) while he was standing before him. I said: "May I be your ransom! This is a child of three years?" He said: "That does not harm him at all; ‘Isa stood as the Proof (الحجة) when he was [only] three years old."

I say: What is mentioned in some other narrations is close to this.

In the same source, with its chain to Mu‘awiyah ibn Wahb, who said: I asked Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام) about the best thing by which servants draw near to their Lord, and what is most beloved of that to Allah (عز وجل)? He said: "I know of nothing after Gnosis (المعرفة) better than this prayer (الصلاة). Do you not see that the righteous servant ‘Isa ibn Maryam said: وَأَوْصَانِي بِالصَّلَاةِ وَالزَّكَاةِ مَا دُمْتُ حَيًّا ‘and He has enjoined me to [maintain] the prayer and to [pay] the zakat as long as I live’ [19:31]?"

In ‘Uyun al-Akhbar, with its chain to Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) in a hadith: "And among them [the major sins] is the mistreatment of parents (عقوق الوالدين), because Allah (عز وجل) has made the one who is undutiful (العاق) a wretched tyrant (جبارا شقيا) in His saying, narrating from ‘Isa (عليه السلام): وَبَرًّا بِوَالِدَتِي وَلَمْ يَجْعَلْنِي جَبَّارًا شَقِيًّا ‘and [He has made me] dutiful to my mother, and He has not made me a wretched tyrant’ [19:32]."

I say: The apparent meaning of the narration is that he (عليه السلام) took his saying: وَلَمْ يَجْعَلْنِي جَبَّارًا شَقِيًّا "and He has not made me a wretched tyrant" as an appositional explanation (عطف تفسير) for his saying: وَبَرًّا بِوَالِدَتِي "and [He has made me] dutiful to my mother."

In al-Majma‘, Muslim narrated in the Sahih with the chain to Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "When the people of Paradise enter Paradise and the people of the Fire enter the Fire, it will be said: ‘O people of Paradise!’ and they will look up and see. And it will be said: ‘O people of the Fire!’ and they will look up and see. Then Death (الموت) will be brought as if it were a salt-and-pepper ram (كبش أملح). It will be said to them: ‘Do you recognize Death?’ They will say: ‘This is it, this is it,’ and everyone will have recognized it." He said:

"Then it will be brought forward and slaughtered. Then it will be said: ‘O people of Paradise, eternity (خلود) and no more death! O people of the Fire, eternity and no more death!’" He said: "That is His saying: وَأَنْذِرْهُمْ يَوْمَ الْحَسْرَةِ ‘Warn them of the Day of Regret’ [19:39]."

He said: Our companions [the Shi‘i scholars] narrated it from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه السلام), then it added at the end: "The people of Paradise will rejoice with such joy that if anyone were to die [of joy] that day, they would die of joy. And the people of the Fire will groan with such a groan that if anyone were to die, they would die."

I say: This meaning was narrated by others besides Muslim among the masters of the collections, such as al-Bukhari, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa’i, al-Tabari, and others, from Abu Sa‘id, Abu Hurayrah, Ibn Mas‘ud, and Ibn ‘Abbas.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: Regarding His saying: إِنَّا نَحْنُ نَرِثُ الْأَرْضَ وَمَنْ عَلَيْهَا "Indeed We shall inherit the earth and whoever is on it" [19:40], he said: "Everything that Allah created, Allah shall inherit on the Day of Resurrection."

I say: This is the second of the two meanings of the verse mentioned previously in its commentary.

Ayahs

وَٱذْكُرْ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ صِدِّيقًا نَّبِيًّا (٤١)
And mention in the Book Abraham. Indeed, he was a truthful man and a prophet. (41)
إِذْ قَالَ لِأَبِيهِ يَـٰٓأَبَتِ لِمَ تَعْبُدُ مَا لَا يَسْمَعُ وَلَا يُبْصِرُ وَلَا يُغْنِى عَنكَ شَيْـًٔا (٤٢)
When he said to his father, ‘Father! Why do you worship that which neither hears nor sees, and is of no avail to you in any way? (42)
يَـٰٓأَبَتِ إِنِّى قَدْ جَآءَنِى مِنَ ٱلْعِلْمِ مَا لَمْ يَأْتِكَ فَٱتَّبِعْنِىٓ أَهْدِكَ صِرَٰطًا سَوِيًّا (٤٣)
Father! Indeed a knowledge has already come to me, which has not come to you. So follow me that I may guide you to a right path. (43)
يَـٰٓأَبَتِ لَا تَعْبُدِ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ ۖ إِنَّ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنَ كَانَ لِلرَّحْمَـٰنِ عَصِيًّا (٤٤)
Father! Do not worship Satan. Indeed Satan is disobedient to the All-beneficent. (44)
يَـٰٓأَبَتِ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ أَن يَمَسَّكَ عَذَابٌ مِّنَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ فَتَكُونَ لِلشَّيْطَـٰنِ وَلِيًّا (٤٥)
Father! I am indeed afraid that a punishment from the All-beneficent will befall you, and you will become Satan’s accomplice.’ (45)
قَالَ أَرَاغِبٌ أَنتَ عَنْ ءَالِهَتِى يَـٰٓإِبْرَٰهِيمُ ۖ لَئِن لَّمْ تَنتَهِ لَأَرْجُمَنَّكَ ۖ وَٱهْجُرْنِى مَلِيًّا (٤٦)
He said, ‘Abraham! Are you renouncing my gods? If you do not desist, I will stone you. Get away from me for a long while.’ (46)
قَالَ سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَيْكَ ۖ سَأَسْتَغْفِرُ لَكَ رَبِّىٓ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ بِى حَفِيًّا (٤٧)
He said, ‘Peace be to you! I shall plead with my Lord to forgive you. Indeed, He is gracious to me. (47)
وَأَعْتَزِلُكُمْ وَمَا تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَأَدْعُوا۟ رَبِّى عَسَىٰٓ أَلَّآ أَكُونَ بِدُعَآءِ رَبِّى شَقِيًّا (٤٨)
I dissociate myself from you and whatever you invoke besides Allah. I will supplicate my Lord. Hopefully, I will not be disappointed in supplicating my Lord.’ (48)
فَلَمَّا ٱعْتَزَلَهُمْ وَمَا يَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَهَبْنَا لَهُۥٓ إِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ ۖ وَكُلًّا جَعَلْنَا نَبِيًّا (٤٩)
So when he had left them and what they worshipped besides Allah, We gave him Isaac and Jacob, and each We made a prophet. (49)
وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُم مِّن رَّحْمَتِنَا وَجَعَلْنَا لَهُمْ لِسَانَ صِدْقٍ عَلِيًّا (٥٠)
And We gave them out of Our mercy, and conferred on them a worthy and lofty repute. (50)

(Commentary)

The verses point to a portion of the story of Ibrahim (peace be upon him), namely his disputation with his father regarding the matter of idols through the primordial guidance (الهدي الفطري) — the innate leading toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — and the certain knowledge (المعرفة اليقينية) that Allah had granted him. It describes his withdrawal from his father, his people, and their deities, whereupon Allah granted him Ishaq and Ya‘qub, distinguished him with a "word" remaining among his progeny, and established for him and his descendants a beautiful and lasting remembrance throughout the ages.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتابِ إِبْراهِيمَ إِنَّهُ كانَ صِدِّيقاً نَبِيًّا — "And mention in the Book Ibrahim. Indeed he was a truthful man, a prophet." (19:41). It appears that the "truthful man" (الصديق) is an intensive form (اسم مبالغة) derived from truthfulness (الصدق). He is one who is superlative in truthfulness, such that he says only what he does and does what he says; there is no contradiction between his word and his deed. Such was Ibrahim (peace be upon him); he proclaimed monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — in a pagan world while he was alone. He disputed with his father and his people, resisted the King of Babylon, broke the idols, and remained firm in what he said until he was cast into the fire. Then he withdrew from them and what they worshipped, just as he had promised his father on the first day. Consequently, Allah granted him Ishaq and Ya‘qub, up to the end of the gifts the Exalted enumerated.

It has been said that "truthful man" (الصديق) is an intensive form of "attestation" (التصديق), meaning he was one who frequently attested to the truth, confirming it through both his speech and his actions. While this meaning aligns with the first in terms of its ultimate result, it is rendered less likely by the rarity of the intensive form being derived from the augmented verb (المزيد فيه).

The term "prophet" (النبي) follows the pattern of fa‘īl and is derived from "news" (النبإ). A prophet is so named because he possesses news of the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — through revelation from Allah. It is also said to be derived from "elevation" (النبوة) in the sense of high status, named thus due to the loftiness of his rank.

His saying, the Exalted: إِذْ قالَ لِأَبِيهِ يا أَبَتِ لِمَ تَعْبُدُ ما لا يَسْمَعُ وَلا يُبْصِرُ وَلا يُغْنِي عَنْكَ شَيْئاً — "When he said to his father, ‘O my father! Why do you worship what neither hears nor sees, and is of no avail to you in any way?" (19:42). The word "when" (إذ) serves as an adverbial qualifier (ظرف) for Ibrahim, as the intent is to mention him and his news and story, similar to what preceded in His saying: وَاذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتابِ مَرْيَمَ (And mention in the Book Maryam). As for the view of those who say it is an adverbial qualifier for "truthful man" (صِدِّيقاً) or "prophet" (نَبِيًّا), it is an affectation that a sound nature finds distasteful.

Ibrahim alerted his father in the discourse he first presented to him: first, that the path he was following by worshipping idols was futile and vain; and second, that he possessed knowledge which his father did not, so he should follow him so that he might guide him to the path of truth, for his father was in danger of the guardianship of Satan (ولاية الشيطان).

Thus, his saying: يا أَبَتِ لِمَ تَعْبُدُ ما لا يَسْمَعُ وَلا يُبْصِرُ (O my father! Why do you worship what neither hears nor sees...) is a reproachful denial (إنكار توبيخي) of his worship of idols. He shifted from mentioning the idols themselves to mentioning their attributes—"what neither hears nor sees"—to point to the evidence within the delivery of the conclusion, providing the proof within the claim. This proof is that the worship of idols is futile and vain from two perspectives:

First: Worship (العبادة) is the manifestation of submission and the representation of humility from the worshipper to the worshipped. This is only sound if the worshipped is aware of it. Idols (الأصنام) are inanimate objects, fashioned forms devoid of consciousness; they neither hear nor see. Thus, worshipping them is futile and has no effect. This is what he pointed to by saying: لا يَسْمَعُ وَلا يُبْصِرُ (neither hears nor sees).

Second: Worship, supplication, and the raising of needs to a thing are only done to bring benefit to the worshipper or to ward off harm. This necessarily depends on the power (قدرة) of the worshipped to do so. Idols have no power over anything; they cannot avail their worshipper in any way by bringing benefit or warding off harm. Thus, worshipping them is futile and has no effect. This is what he pointed to by saying: وَلا يُغْنِي عَنْكَ شَيْئاً (and is of no avail to you in any way).

It has already been mentioned in the commentary of Surah al-An‘am that the person Ibrahim (peace be upon him) addressed as "O my father" (يا أَبَتِ) was not his biological father, but rather his uncle, his maternal grandfather, or his mother's husband after his father's death; so refer back to that.

The well-known view among grammarians regarding the expression "O my father" (يا أبت) is that the tā’ is a substitute for the first-person possessive pronoun yā’, and similarly "O my mother" (يا أمت). This substitution is specific to the vocative (النداء); one does not say, for example, "my father said" (قال أبت) or "my mother said" (قالت أمت).

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَبَتِ إِنِّي قَدْ جاءَنِي مِنَ الْعِلْمِ ما لَمْ يَأْتِكَ فَاتَّبِعْنِي أَهْدِكَ صِراطاً سَوِيًّا — "O my father! Indeed there has come to me a knowledge that has not come to you. So follow me, I will guide you to a right path." (19:43). When he explained to him the falsehood and futility of his idol worship—the necessary implication being that he was following an uneven path out of ignorance—he alerted him that he possessed knowledge regarding this matter which the father did not. Therefore, the father ought to follow him so that he might guide him to a path (صراط)—which is the way whose traveler does not stray due to its clarity—that is "right" (سوي), a matter of which the father was heedless. For this reason, he used the indefinite form when he said: أَهْدِكَ صِراطاً سَوِيًّا (I will guide you to a right path) rather than saying "the right path" (الصراط السوي). It is as if he were saying: "Since you are following a path—and follow a path you must—do not follow this uneven path out of ignorance; rather, follow me, and I will guide you to a right path, for I possess knowledge of this matter."

In his saying: قَدْ جاءَنِي مِنَ الْعِلْمِ (there has come to me a knowledge) there is evidence that he was granted knowledge of the truth before this call and disputation. This confirms what we previously presented in his story in Surah al-An‘am: that he was granted knowledge of Allah and the witnessing of the kingdom of the heavens and the earth before he met his father and his people to dispute with them.

The meaning of guidance in his saying: أَهْدِكَ صِراطاً سَوِيًّا (I will guide you to a right path) is guidance (الهداية) in the sense of "showing the way" rather than "conveying to the goal," for the latter is the function of the Imam, and he had not yet been appointed an Imam. We have detailed the discussion on this meaning in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: قالَ إِنِّي جاعِلُكَ لِلنَّاسِ إِماماً — "He said, ‘I am making you the Imam of mankind.’" (2:124).

His saying, the Exalted: يا أَبَتِ لا تَعْبُدِ الشَّيْطانَ إِنَّ الشَّيْطانَ كانَ لِلرَّحْمنِ عَصِيًّا — "O my father! Do not worship Satan. Indeed Satan is disobedient to the All-beneficent." (19:44), until the end of the two verses. The pagans believed in the existence of the Jinn—and Iblis is from the Jinn—and they worshipped their idols just as they worshipped the idols of the angels and the saintly among humans. However, the prohibition here does not mean prohibition of worship in that specific sense, as there would be no reason to single out the Jinn from among their other deities for such a prohibition. Rather, worship (العبادة) here means obedience (الطاعة), as in His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يا بَنِي آدَمَ أَنْ لا تَعْبُدُوا الشَّيْطانَ — "Did I not exhort you, O children of Adam, saying, ‘Do not worship Satan.’" (36:60). Thus, the prohibition against worshipping Satan is a prohibition against obeying him in what he commands, and among the things he commands is the worship of other than Allah.

When he called him to follow him so that he might guide him to a right path, he wanted to incite him toward following by uprooting him from his current state. He alerted him that the worship of idols is not merely a futile act that neither harms nor benefits; rather, it is liable to lead its practitioner to the place of destruction and bring him under the guardianship of Satan (ولاية الشيطان), after which there is no hope for rectification or success, nor any prospect of safety or happiness.

This is because worshipping them—while the One entitled to worship is Allah, the Glorified, by virtue of His being the All-Beneficent (الرحمن) from Whom every mercy originates—and seeking proximity to them is only from Satan and his embellishment. Satan is disobedient (عصي) to the All-Beneficent; he does not command anything that contains His pleasure, but only whispers that which involves His disobedience, leading to His punishment and wrath. Persisting in His disobedience, especially regarding His most exclusive right—which is His worship alone—carries the fear that His mercy, which is guidance to happiness, will be cut off from the sinner, and the punishment of abandonment (عذاب الخذلان) will descend upon him. Thus, Allah will not take charge of his affairs, and Satan will become his master (مولاه) and he will be the protégé of Satan (ولي الشيطان), which is utter destruction.

The meaning of the two verses—and Allah knows best—is: "O my father, do not obey Satan in what he commands you regarding the worship of idols, because Satan is a rebel persistently disobeying Allah, Who is the source of every mercy and blessing. He commands nothing except that which involves His disobedience and deprivation of His mercy. I only forbid you from His disobedience in obeying Satan because I fear, O my father, that some of the punishment of His abandonment will seize you and His mercy will be cut off from you, leaving no one to take charge of your affairs except Satan; thus you would become a protégé of Satan, and Satan would be your master."

From what has preceded, several points have become clear:

First: The meaning of worship in His saying: لا تَعْبُدِ الشَّيْطانَ (Do not worship Satan) is the worship of obedience. The description of Satan (الشيطان)—meaning the "evil one"—plays a role in the ruling.

Second: The reason for substituting the Name of Majesty (Allah) with the attribute "the All-Beneficent" (الرحمن) in two places. The attribute of absolute mercy has a role in both rulings: His being the source of every mercy and blessing is what necessitates the foulness of persisting in His disobedience and justifies the prohibition of obeying one who persists in rebelling against Him. Similarly, His being the source of every mercy is the cause for the fear of His punishment, which entails the withholding of mercy and the onset of vengeance and wretchedness.

Third: The intended meaning of "punishment" (العذاب) is abandonment (الخذلان), or something of that meaning, such as the withholding of mercy and leaving the human to his own soul. What some have mentioned—that it refers to the punishment of the Hereafter—is not supported by the context.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ أَراغِبٌ أَنْتَ عَنْ آلِهَتِي يا إِبْراهِيمُ لَئِنْ لَمْ تَنْتَهِ لَأَرْجُمَنَّكَ وَاهْجُرْنِي مَلِيًّا — "He said, ‘Are you turning away from my gods, O Ibrahim? If you do not desist, I will surely stone you. Now leave me for a long time.’" (19:46). Turning away from something (الرغبة عن الشيء) is the opposite of desiring it (الرغبة فيه), as stated in al-Majma‘. Desisting (الانتهاء) is to cease an action after being forbidden. Stoning (الرجم) is the throwing of stones, and its well-known meaning is killing by throwing stones. Forsaking (الهجر) is leaving and parting. "A long time" (الملي) refers to a long duration.

In the verse is a threat to Ibrahim with the most disgraceful and humiliating form of killing, which is stoning—the method by which outcasts are killed. It also contains Azar’s expulsion of Ibrahim from his presence.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ سَلامٌ عَلَيْكَ سَأَسْتَغْفِرُ لَكَ رَبِّي إِنَّهُ كانَ بِي حَفِيًّا — "He said, ‘Peace be to you! I will plead with my Lord to forgive you. Indeed He is gracious to me.’" (19:47). The "gracious" (الحفي), according to what al-Raghib mentioned, is the kind and subtle benefactor who tracks the minute details of needs, acting with excellence and fulfilling them one after another. It is said: hafa yahfu hafiyyan and hafwatan. Ihfa’ al-su’al (insistence in asking) means persistence and thoroughness in it.

Ibrahim (peace be upon him) responded to his father’s mistreatment and threat—which included the removal of safety from him—with "peace" (السلام), which contains kindness and the granting of safety. He promised to seek forgiveness for him from his Lord and to withdraw from them and what they invoked besides Allah, just as his father had ordered him to leave him for a long time.

As for the "peace," it is the habit of the noble; he countered his father’s ignorance with it when he threatened him with stoning and expelled him for a word of truth he had spoken. The Exalted said: وَإِذا مَرُّوا بِاللَّغْوِ مَرُّوا كِراماً — "and when they pass by vain talk, they pass by nobly." (25:72), and He said: وَإِذا خاطَبَهُمُ الْجاهِلُونَ قالُوا سَلاماً — "and when the ignorant address them, they say, ‘Peace!’" (25:63). As for the claim that it was a peace of farewell and a greeting of parting and migration in compliance with his saying: "Now leave me for a long time," it is countered by the fact that he only withdrew from him and his people after a period that was not short.

Regarding his seeking forgiveness for his father while he was a polytheist, the apparent meaning of his saying: يا أَبَتِ إِنِّي أَخافُ أَنْ يَمَسَّكَ عَذابٌ مِنَ الرَّحْمنِ فَتَكُونَ لِلشَّيْطانِ وَلِيًّا (O my father! Indeed I fear that a punishment from the All-beneficent will visit you, and you will become a protégé of Satan) is that he (peace be upon him) was not at that time certain that his father was among the protégés of Satan—meaning one whose heart was sealed with polytheism, a denier, obstinate against the truth, and an enemy of Allah, the Glorified. Had he been certain, he would not have expressed it with "Indeed I fear"; rather, it was possible that he was ignorant and oppressed (مستضعف), such that if the truth were made clear to him, he would follow it. It is possible for divine mercy to encompass such people. The Exalted said: إِلَّا الْمُسْتَضْعَفِينَ مِنَ الرِّجالِ وَالنِّساءِ وَالْوِلْدانِ لا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ حِيلَةً وَلا يَهْتَدُونَ سَبِيلاً فَأُولئِكَ عَسَى اللهُ أَنْ يَعْفُوَ عَنْهُمْ وَكانَ اللهُ عَفُوًّا غَفُوراً — "except the oppressed among men, women and children, who have neither the resources nor are they guided to a way. Maybe Allah will pardon them, and Allah is all-excusing, all-forgiving." (4:98-99). Thus, he (peace be upon him) sought to win him over with the promise of seeking forgiveness, without making forgiveness a certainty for him; rather, he expressed hope, as evidenced by his saying: إِنَّهُ كانَ بِي حَفِيًّا (Indeed He is gracious to me) and the saying of the Exalted: إِلَّا قَوْلَ إِبْراهِيمَ لِأَبِيهِ لَأَسْتَغْفِرَنَّ لَكَ وَما أَمْلِكُ لَكَ مِنَ اللهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ — "except for Ibrahim’s saying to his father, ‘I will surely plead for forgiveness for you, though I cannot avail you anything against Allah.’" (60:4).

This is supported by the saying of the Exalted: ما كانَ لِلنَّبِيِّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنْ يَسْتَغْفِرُوا لِلْمُشْرِكِينَ وَلَوْ كانُوا أُولِي قُرْبى مِنْ بَعْدِ ما تَبَيَّنَ لَهُمْ أَنَّهُمْ أَصْحابُ الْجَحِيمِ ، وَما كانَ اسْتِغْفارُ إِبْراهِيمَ لِأَبِيهِ إِلَّا عَنْ مَوْعِدَةٍ وَعَدَها إِيَّاهُ فَلَمَّا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُ أَنَّهُ عَدُوٌّ لِلَّهِ تَبَرَّأَ مِنْهُ إِنَّ إِبْراهِيمَ لَأَوَّاهٌ حَلِيمٌ — "The Prophet and the faithful may not plead for the forgiveness of the polytheists, even if they should be [their] relatives, after it has become clear to them that they are the inmates of hell. Ibrahim’s pleading for his father’s forgiveness was only due to a promise he had made him. So when it became clear to him that he was an enemy of Allah, he repudiated him. Indeed Ibrahim was most tender-hearted and forbearing." (9:113-114). His repudiation of him after his enmity became clear is evidence that before that, at the time of the promise, he hoped he was not an enemy of Allah despite being a polytheist. This applies only to the ignorant person who is not obstinate.

This view is further supported by the saying of the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَتَّخِذُوا عَدُوِّي وَعَدُوَّكُمْ أَوْلِياءَ تُلْقُونَ إِلَيْهِمْ بِالْمَوَدَّةِ — "O you who have faith! Do not take My enemy and your enemy for friends, showing them affection..." (60:1), until He says: لا يَنْهاكُمُ اللهُ عَنِ الَّذِينَ لَمْ يُقاتِلُوكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ وَلَمْ يُخْرِجُوكُمْ مِنْ دِيارِكُمْ أَنْ تَبَرُّوهُمْ وَتُقْسِطُوا إِلَيْهِمْ إِنَّ اللهُ يُحِبُّ الْمُقْسِطِينَ — "Allah does not forbid you in regard to those who did not make war against you on account of religion and did not expel you from your homes, that you deal with them with kindness and justice. Indeed Allah loves the just." (60:8).

Among the explanations offered for his seeking forgiveness for his father while he was a polytheist is that he promised him forgiveness and sought it for him based on the dictates of reason (العقل), for reason does not preclude its possibility; it was only the transmitted law (النقل) that prohibited it, and the prohibition had not been established in the law at that time. Then, when it was forbidden in his law, he repudiated him.

However, this does not align with the verses of the story, as becomes clear upon reflecting on what we have presented.

Another explanation is that his seeking forgiveness was conditional upon his father's repentance and faith. This is as you see (weak).

Another is that the meaning of "I will plead with my Lord to forgive you" (سَأَسْتَغْفِرُ لَكَ رَبِّي) is: "I will pray to Allah not to punish you in this world." This, like the one before it, is a restriction without a restrictor.

Another is that he promised to pray for the effect (forgiveness), which by implication is a promise to pray for the cause. Thus, the meaning of "I will ask Allah to forgive you" is: "I will ask Him to grant you success in repenting and to guide you to faith so that He may forgive you." It is possible to make the request for forgiveness a metaphor (كناية) for requesting the success of repentance and guidance to faith.

While this is the most balanced of the views, it is not without some remoteness, because the speech contains an attempt to win him over, and this is more suited to a request for forgiveness than a request for success and guidance. Reflect upon this.

Similar to his prayer for his father is his prayer for the generality of the polytheists in His saying: وَاجْنُبْنِي وَبَنِيَّ أَنْ نَعْبُدَ الْأَصْنامَ رَبِّ إِنَّهُنَّ أَضْلَلْنَ كَثِيراً مِنَ النَّاسِ فَمَنْ تَبِعَنِي فَإِنَّهُ مِنِّي وَمَنْ عَصانِي فَإِنَّكَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ — "and keep me and my children away from worshipping idols. My Lord! Indeed they have misled many people. So whoever follows me indeed belongs to me, and as for him who disobeys me, well, You are indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful." (14:35-36).

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَعْتَزِلُكُمْ وَما تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ وَأَدْعُوا رَبِّي عَسى أَلَّا أَكُونَ بِدُعاءِ رَبِّي شَقِيًّا — "I will withdraw from you and what you invoke besides Allah, and I will supplicate my Lord. Hopefully, I will not be disappointed in supplicating my Lord." (19:48). This is a promise to withdraw from them and distance himself from them and their idols, so that he may be alone with his Lord and sincere in his supplication to Him, hoping that he will not be wretched (شقياً) because of his supplication. He expressed this as a hope (عسى) because these causes—such as supplication, turning toward Allah, and the like—are not causes that necessitate anything upon the Exalted; rather, rewarding and granting happiness and the like are purely out of His grace. Furthermore, matters are judged by their ends, and none knows the Unseen except Allah; thus, the believer must walk between fear and hope.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا اعْتَزَلَهُمْ وَما يَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ وَهَبْنا لَهُ إِسْحاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ — "So when he had withdrawn from them and what they worshipped besides Allah, We gave him Ishaq and Ya‘qub..." (19:49), until the end of the two verses. Perhaps the restriction to mentioning Ishaq is because the purpose was to mention the continuity of prophethood in the Israelite tree; therefore, Ishaq was followed by the mention of Ya‘qub, for in his lineage was a vast multitude of prophets. This is also supported by His saying: وَكُلًّا جَعَلْنا نَبِيًّا (and each We made a prophet).

His saying: وَوَهَبْنا لَهُمْ مِنْ رَحْمَتِنا (And We gave them of Our mercy) could possibly refer to the Imamate (الإمامة) — the divine leadership of the community — as occurred in His saying: وَوَهَبْنا لَهُ إِسْحاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ نافِلَةً وَكُلًّا جَعَلْنا صالِحِينَ وَجَعَلْناهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنا — "And We gave him Ishaq, and Ya‘qub as a grandson, and each We made righteous. We made them imams, guiding by Our command..." (21:72-73). Or it could refer to support by the Holy Spirit (روح القدس), as indicated by His saying: وَأَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْهِمْ فِعْلَ الْخَيْراتِ (and We revealed to them the doing of good works) according to what will come regarding its meaning, or it could refer to absolute divine guardianship (الولاية الإلهية).

His saying: وَجَعَلْنا لَهُمْ لِسانَ صِدْقٍ عَلِيًّا (and We appointed for them a high worthy mention) — "the tongue" (اللسان), according to what they have mentioned, is the mention of a person among people, whether in praise or blame. When it is annexed to "truth" (الصدق), it refers to beautiful praise in which there is no falsehood. "High" (العلي) means lofty. The meaning is: "We established for them a beautiful, truthful praise of lofty status."

Ayahs

وَٱذْكُرْ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مُوسَىٰٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ مُخْلَصًا وَكَانَ رَسُولًا نَّبِيًّا (٥١)
And mention in the Book Moses. Indeed he was exclusively dedicated [to Allah], and an apostle and prophet. (51)
وَنَـٰدَيْنَـٰهُ مِن جَانِبِ ٱلطُّورِ ٱلْأَيْمَنِ وَقَرَّبْنَـٰهُ نَجِيًّا (٥٢)
We called him from the right side of the Mount and We drew him near [to Ourselves] for confidential discourse. (52)
وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُۥ مِن رَّحْمَتِنَآ أَخَاهُ هَـٰرُونَ نَبِيًّا (٥٣)
And We gave him out of Our mercy his brother Aaron, a prophet. (53)
وَٱذْكُرْ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ إِسْمَـٰعِيلَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ صَادِقَ ٱلْوَعْدِ وَكَانَ رَسُولًا نَّبِيًّا (٥٤)
And mention in the Book Ishmael. Indeed, he was true to his promise, and an apostle and prophet. (54)
وَكَانَ يَأْمُرُ أَهْلَهُۥ بِٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَٱلزَّكَوٰةِ وَكَانَ عِندَ رَبِّهِۦ مَرْضِيًّا (٥٥)
He used to bid his family to [maintain] the prayer and to [pay] the zakat, and was pleasing to his Lord. (55)
وَٱذْكُرْ فِى ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ إِدْرِيسَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ صِدِّيقًا نَّبِيًّا (٥٦)
And mention in the Book Idrees. Indeed, he was a truthful one and a prophet, (56)
وَرَفَعْنَـٰهُ مَكَانًا عَلِيًّا (٥٧)
and We raised him to an exalted station. (57)

(Commentary)

The text mentions another group of prophets and some of the gift of mercy (موهبة الرحمة) with which Allah specially endowed them; they are Moses (موسى), Aaron (هارون), Ishmael (إسماعيل), and Idris (إدريس), peace be upon them (عليه‌السلام).

His saying, the Exalted: وَاذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتابِ مُوسى إِنَّهُ كانَ مُخْلَصاً وَكانَ رَسُولاً نَبِيًّا — "And mention in the Book Moses. Indeed he was dedicated (مُخْلَصاً), and he was an apostle (رَسُولاً), a prophet (نَبِيًّا)." (19:51). The meaning of the "dedicated" (المُخْلَص) — the one whom Allah has purified and chosen exclusively for Himself such that no one else has a share in him, neither in his soul nor in his actions — has been previously explained, and it is the highest station of servitude (العبودية). The distinction between the messenger (الرسول) and the prophet (النبي) has also been previously discussed.

His saying, the Exalted: وَنادَيْناهُ مِنْ جانِبِ الطُّورِ الْأَيْمَنِ وَقَرَّبْناهُ نَجِيًّا — "We called him from the right side of the Mount (الطُّورِ), and We brought him near for confidential discourse (نَجِيًّا)." (19:52). "The right" (الأيمن) is an adjective for "side" (جانب), meaning the right side of the Mount. In al-Majma‘, it is stated: "The confidant" (النجي) is in the sense of one engaged in intimate discourse (المناجي), similar to "companion" (الجليس) and "bedfellow" (الضجيع).

It is evident that his being "brought near" (تقريبه), peace be upon him, was a spiritual nearness (تقريبا معنويا), even if this divine gift occurred in a physical place, which was the Mount (الطور), where the speaking (التكليم) took place. An analogy from the sensory realm is when a mighty master calls his humble servant and brings him close to his assembly until he makes him a confidant with whom he speaks privately; therein is the attainment of that to which others have no access.

His saying, the Exalted: وَوَهَبْنا لَهُ مِنْ رَحْمَتِنا أَخاهُ هارُونَ نَبِيًّا — "And We gave him out of Our mercy his brother Aaron, a prophet." (19:53). This is an allusion to the answering of the prayer Moses made when he was first given revelation at the Mount, when he said: وَاجْعَلْ لِي وَزِيراً مِنْ أَهْلِي هارُونَ أَخِي اشْدُدْ بِهِ أَزْرِي وَأَشْرِكْهُ فِي أَمْرِي — "Appoint for me a minister from my family, Aaron, my brother. Strengthen my back through him, and make him associate in my affair." (20:29-32).

His saying, the Exalted: وَاذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتابِ إِسْماعِيلَ إِنَّهُ كانَ صادِقَ الْوَعْدِ — "And mention in the Book Ishmael. Indeed he was true to his promise..." (19:54) until the end of the two verses. They have differed regarding this "Ishmael" (إِسْماعِيلَ). The majority (الجمهور) say he is Ishmael, the son of Abraham, the Friend of the All-Beneficent (خليل الرحمن); he was mentioned alone and not with Isaac and Jacob out of special regard for his status. Others say he is someone else, namely Ishmael the son of Ezekiel, one of the prophets of the Children of Israel; they argue that had he been the son of Abraham, he would have been mentioned with Isaac and Jacob.

The justification given for the majority view—that he was mentioned independently out of special regard—is weakened by the fact that if it were so, it would have been more appropriate to mention him after Abraham and before Moses (عليه‌السلام), not after Moses.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكانَ يَأْمُرُ أَهْلَهُ بِالصَّلاةِ وَالزَّكاةِ وَكانَ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِ مَرْضِيًّا — "He used to bid his family to prayer and charity, and he was pleasing to his Lord." (19:55). What is meant by "his family" (أَهْلَهُ) is his close kin from his progeny, his clan, and his people, as is the apparent meaning of the word. It has been said that "his family" means his community (أمته), but this is a claim without evidence.

The meaning of his being "pleasing to his Lord" (عِنْدَ رَبِّهِ مَرْضِيًّا) is that his very soul was pleasing, rather than just his actions, as some have interpreted it; for the absolute nature of the expression does not suit restricting the pleasure (الرضا) to actions alone.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتابِ إِدْرِيسَ إِنَّهُ كانَ صِدِّيقاً نَبِيًّا — "And mention in the Book Idris. Indeed he was a truthful one, a prophet." (19:56) until the end of the two verses. They say that the Prophet Idris's name was Enoch (أخنوخ), and he was one of the ancestors of Noah (عليه‌السلام) according to what is mentioned in the Book of Genesis in the Torah. He became famous as "Idris" due to his extensive engagement in study (الدرس).

His saying: وَرَفَعْناهُ مَكاناً عَلِيًّا — "And We raised him to a station sublime." (19:57). It is possible to infer from the context of the stories narrated in the Surah—which enumerate the gifts of prophethood (النبوة) and guardianship (الولاية), which are spiritual divine stations (مقامات إلهية معنوية)—that the "sublime station" (المكان العلي) to which he was raised is a degree among the degrees of nearness (القرب); for there is no inherent merit in physical elevation (الارتفاع المادي) or ascending to the far reaches of the atmosphere, wherever that may be.

It has also been said—as mentioned in some narrations—that the meaning is that Allah raised him to one of the heavens and took his soul there. In this, there would be the showing of a miraculous sign (آية خارقة) and a profound divine power, which is merit enough.

(The Story of Ishmael, the Truthful to the Promise)

The story of the Prophet Ishmael, son of Ezekiel, does not appear in the Quran except in these two verses, according to one of the two interpretations. Allah, the Glorified, has praised him with beautiful praise, counting him as one who was true to his promise (صادق الوعد), a bidder of the right (آمرا بالمعروف), and pleasing to his Lord, and mentioned that he was an apostle, a prophet.

As for the narrations, it is recorded in ‘Ilal al-Shara’i‘, with its chain of transmission to Ibn Abi ‘Umayr and Muhammad ibn Sinan, from someone who mentioned it from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه‌السلام), who said: إن إسماعيل الذي قال الله عز وجل في كتابه : «وَاذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتابِ إِسْماعِيلَ إِنَّهُ كانَ صادِقَ الْوَعْدِ ـ وَكانَ رَسُولاً نَبِيًّا» لم يكن إسماعيل بن إبراهيم ـ بل كان نبيا من الأنبياء بعثه الله عز وجل إلى قومه ـ فأخذوه وسلخوا فروة رأسه ووجهه ـ فأتاه ملك فقال إن الله جل جلاله بعثني إليك ـ فمرني بما شئت فقال : لي أسوة بما يصنع بالأنبياء عليهم‌السلام. — "Indeed, the Ishmael of whom Allah, Mighty and Majestic, said in His Book: 'And mention in the Book Ishmael. Indeed he was true to his promise, and he was an apostle, a prophet,' was not Ishmael the son of Abraham. Rather, he was a prophet among the prophets whom Allah, Mighty and Majestic, sent to his people. They seized him and flayed the skin of his head and face. An angel came to him and said: 'Indeed Allah, Majestic is His Majesty, has sent me to you, so command me as you wish.' He replied: 'I have an example in what is done to the prophets (عليه‌السلام).'"

I say: This meaning is also narrated with its chain from Abu Basir from him (عليه‌السلام), and at its end: يكون لي أسوة بالحسين عليه‌السلام — "I shall have an example in al-Husayn (عليه‌السلام)."

In al-‘Uyun, with its chain to Sulayman al-Ja‘fari from Abu al-Hasan al-Rida (عليه‌السلام), who said: أتدري لم سمي إسماعيل صادق الوعد؟ قال : قلت : لا أدري. قال : وعد رجلا فجلس له حولا ينتظره. — "Do you know why Ishmael was called 'truthful to the promise'?" He said: "I said: 'I do not know.'" He said: "He made a promise to a man and sat for him for a year (حولا) waiting for him."

I say: This meaning is narrated in al-Kafi from Ibn Abi ‘Umayr from Mansur ibn Hazim from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه‌السلام), and it is also narrated in al-Majma‘ as a detached (مرسلا) report from him (عليه‌السلام).

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying: وَاذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتابِ إِسْماعِيلَ إِنَّهُ كانَ صادِقَ الْوَعْدِ, he said: وعد وعدا فانتظر صاحبه سنة ، وهو إسماعيل بن حزقيل. — "He made a promise and waited for his companion for a year, and he is Ishmael the son of Ezekiel."

I say: His promise (عليه‌السلام)—which was to remain in his place waiting for his companion—was absolute (مطلقا); he did not restrict it to an hour or a day or the like. Thus, the station of truthfulness (مقام الصدق) required him to fulfill it in its absolute sense and to restrain himself in the place where he promised his companion he would stay until he returned to him.

The attribute of fulfillment (الوفاء), like all other psychological attributes (الصفات النفسانية) such as love, will, resolve, faith, trust, and submission, has different levels (مراتب) according to the level of knowledge and certainty (اليقين). Just as there is a level of faith (الإيمان) that can coexist with sin—which is its lowest level—and it continues to grow and purify until it is free from all hidden polytheism (شرك خفي) such that the heart does not attach to anything other than Allah, even by a glance toward what is besides Him—which is its highest level—likewise, fulfillment of a promise has levels. Among its levels in common speech, for example, is staying for an hour or two until another need arises that necessitates leaving, and this is what is conventionally considered fulfillment. A higher level is staying in the place until one normally despairs of the friend's return, such as with the coming of night, thereby qualifying the absolute nature of the promise. A higher level still is adhering to the absolute literalness of the word and staying until he returns, even if the time is long. Strong souls that watch over their words and deeds do not utter a word except what they are capable of making true through action; then, once they have spoken, no distraction turns them away from completing the word and executing the resolve.

In a narration: أن النبي صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله وعد بعض أصحابه بمكة أن ينتظره عند الكعبة ـ حتى يرجع إليه فمضى الرجل لشأنه ونسي الأمر ـ فبقي صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله ثلاثة أيام هناك ينتظره ـ فاطلع بعض الناس عليه فأخبر الرجل بذلك فجاء واعتذر إليه ـ وهذا مقام الصديقين لا يقولون إلا ما يفعلون. — "The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) promised one of his companions in Mecca that he would wait for him at the Kaaba until he returned. The man went about his business and forgot the matter. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) remained there for three days waiting for him. Some people noticed him and informed the man, who then came and apologized to him. This is the station of the truthful (الصديقين); they do not say except what they do."

(The Story of the Prophet Idris (عليه‌السلام))

1 — He (عليه‌السلام) is not mentioned in the Quran except in the two verses of Surah Maryam: وَاذْكُرْ فِي الْكِتابِ إِدْرِيسَ إِنَّهُ كانَ صِدِّيقاً نَبِيًّا وَرَفَعْناهُ مَكاناً عَلِيًّا — "And mention in the Book Idris. Indeed he was a truthful one, a prophet. And We raised him to a station sublime." (19:56-57), and in His saying: وَإِسْماعِيلَ وَإِدْرِيسَ وَذَا الْكِفْلِ كُلٌّ مِنَ الصَّابِرِينَ وَأَدْخَلْناهُمْ فِي رَحْمَتِنا إِنَّهُمْ مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ — "And Ishmael, Idris, and Dhul-Kifl—each was among the patient. And We admitted them into Our mercy. Indeed they were among the righteous." (21:85-86).

In these verses is beautiful praise from Him, the Exalted, for him; He counted him as a prophet, a truthful one (صديقا), among the patient (الصابرين), and among the righteous (الصالحين), and informed that He raised him to a sublime station.

2 — Among the narrations regarding his story is what is in the book Kamal al-Din wa Tamam al-Ni‘mah, with its chain to Ibrahim ibn Abi al-Bilad from his father from Imam al-Baqir (عليه‌السلام)—and the narration is long, we have summarized it: "The beginning of Idris's (عليه‌السلام) prophethood was that in his time there was a tyrannical king. One day he went out for recreation and passed by a lush, green land that pleased him, so he wished to possess it. The land belonged to a believing servant, so he ordered him to be brought and bargained with him to buy it, but he would not sell it nor was he satisfied with the offer. The king returned to the city grieved and bewildered. He consulted a wife of his whom he used to consult in major affairs. She advised him to set up witnesses against the man that he had left the king's religion, so he could kill him and take his land. He did as she advised and usurped the land.

Then Allah revealed to Idris to go to the king and say to him on His behalf: 'Were you not satisfied with killing My believing servant unjustly until you seized his land for yourself and left his family after him in need and hunger? By My Might, I shall surely take vengeance for him from you in the Hereafter, and I shall surely strip you of your kingdom in this world, and I shall surely destroy your city, and I shall surely humiliate your might, and I shall surely feed the dogs the flesh of your wife. For My forbearance toward you has deceived you, O afflicted one.'

So Idris came to him with Allah's message and delivered it in the presence of his companions. The king expelled him from his assembly, then, at his wife's suggestion, sent a group to kill him. Some of Idris's companions became aware of this and advised him to leave and migrate. He left that day with some of his companions, then he communed with his Lord and complained to Him of what he faced from the king. Allah revealed to him to leave the village, and that He would execute His command upon the king and fulfill His word regarding him. Then Idris asked that the sky not rain upon the village and its surroundings until he asked for it, and his request was granted.

Idris informed his companions among the believers of this and ordered them to leave, so they left and scattered throughout the lands—they were twenty men. The news of his revelation and departure spread among the people. He went aside to a cave in a high mountain, worshipping Allah therein, fasting by day, and an angel would bring him food with which he would break his fast every evening.

Allah executed upon the king, his wife, and his city what He had revealed to Idris. Another rebellious tyrant appeared in the city, and the sky withheld its rain from them for twenty years until they were exhausted and their condition became severe. When the exhaustion reached its peak, they mentioned to one another that the hardship they faced was only due to Idris's prayer against them that they should not have rain until they asked him, and his departure from among them while they did not know where he was. They decided that the best course was to return and repent to Allah and ask Him for rain, for He is more merciful to them than Idris. So they gathered for prayer and supplication.

Allah revealed to Idris: 'The people have cried out to Me with repentance, seeking forgiveness, weeping, and supplication. I have had mercy on them, and nothing prevents Me from raining upon them except your request that I not rain upon them until you ask Me. So ask Me that I may relieve them.' Idris said: 'O Allah, I do not ask You.'

Then Allah revealed to the angel who used to bring him food to withhold it from him. He withheld it for three days until hunger overcame him. He cried out: 'O Allah, You have withheld my provision from me before You take my soul!' Allah revealed to him: 'O Idris, you are distressed because I withheld your food for three days, yet you were not distressed by the hunger of the people of your village and their hardship for twenty years? Then I asked you to ask Me to rain upon them, but you were stingy and did not ask. So I have disciplined you with hunger. Descend from your place and seek livelihood for yourself, for I have entrusted you in seeking it to your own resourcefulness.'

Idris descended to a village there and saw a house from which smoke was rising. He entered it, and there was an old woman preparing two loaves of bread on a pan. He asked her to feed him, for hunger had exhausted him. She said: 'O servant of Allah, the prayer of Idris has left us no surplus to feed anyone,' and she swore she possessed nothing else. 'Seek livelihood from other than the people of this village.' He said to her: 'Feed me that which will sustain my soul and support my legs until I seek.' She said: 'They are two loaves, one for me and the other for my son. If I feed you my portion, I will die; and if I feed you my son's portion, he will die; and there is no surplus here.' He said: 'Your son is small, half a loaf is enough for him; so feed each of us a half so it may be a sustenance for us.' She agreed and did so.

When her son saw Idris eating from his loaf, he became agitated until he died. His mother said: 'O servant of Allah, you have killed my son out of distress over his food!' He said: 'Do not be distressed, for I shall revive him for you now by Allah's permission.' He took the boy's arms and said: 'O soul departing from his body by Allah's command, return to his body by Allah's permission, for I am Idris the Prophet.' The boy's soul returned to him.

When his mother heard Idris's words and his saying 'I am Idris,' and saw her son alive, she said: 'I bear witness that you are Idris the Prophet.' She went out crying at the top of her voice in the village: 'Rejoice at the relief, for Idris has entered your village!' Idris went on until he sat at the site of the city of the first tyrant—which had turned into mounds of dust. People from his village gathered to him, seeking his mercy and asking him to pray for them so they might have rain. He said: 'No, not until this tyrant of yours and all the people of your village come to me walking barefoot and ask me for that.'

The tyrant heard of this and sent forty men to Idris, ordering them to bring him. When they came and demanded he go with them, he prayed against them and they all died. Then he sent five hundred men. When they came, they demanded he go and sought his mercy. He showed them the places where their companions had fallen and said: 'I am not going to him, nor asking, until he and all the people of the village come to me walking barefoot and ask me to pray for rain.'

They went back to him and informed him of what he said, and asked him to go to him with all the people of the village walking barefoot and ask him to ask Allah to rain. So they came to him until they stood before him, humble and submissive, and asked him to ask Allah to rain upon them. At that, Idris prayed that the sky rain upon them. A cloud from the sky shaded them, thundered, lightened, and poured down upon them that very hour until they thought it was a flood. They did not return to their homes until they were preoccupied with the water."

In al-Kafi, with its chain to ‘Abdullah ibn Aban from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه‌السلام), in a narration mentioning Masjid al-Sahla: أما علمت أنه موضع بيت إدريس النبي ـ الذي كان يخيط فيه. — "Do you not know that it is the site of the house of the Prophet Idris, in which he used to sew?"

I say: It has become common among historians and biographers that he (عليه‌السلام) was the first to write with a pen and the first to sew.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, it is said: "He was named Idris because of his extensive study (دراسته) of books."

I say: It is mentioned in some narrations regarding the meaning of His saying, the Exalted, concerning Idris (عليه‌السلام): وَرَفَعْناهُ مَكاناً عَلِيًّا — that Allah was angry with one of the angels, so He cut off his wing and cast him onto an island in the sea, where he remained as long as Allah willed. When Allah sent Idris, that angel came to him and asked him to pray to Allah to be pleased with him and return his wing. Idris prayed for him, so Allah returned his wing and was pleased with him.

The angel said to Idris: "Do you have a need?" He said: "Yes, I would like you to raise me to the heaven so I may look at the Angel of Death, for life is not pleasant for me while remembering him." The angel took him on his wing until he reached the fourth heaven. There he found the Angel of Death shaking his head in wonder. Idris greeted him and said: "Why are you shaking your head?" He said: "Indeed, the Lord of Might commanded me to take your soul between the fourth and fifth heavens. I said: 'O Lord, how can this be when between me and him are four heavens, and the thickness of each heaven is a journey of five hundred years, and between every two heavens is a journey of five hundred years?'" Then he took his soul between the fourth and fifth heavens, and that is His saying, the Exalted: وَرَفَعْناهُ مَكاناً عَلِيًّا.

‘Ali ibn Ibrahim al-Qummi narrated this in his Tafsir from his father from Ibn Abi ‘Umayr from someone who narrated it from Abu ‘Abdillah (عليه‌السلام). A similar meaning is narrated in al-Kafi from ‘Ali ibn Ibrahim from his father from ‘Amr ibn ‘Uthman from Mufaddal ibn Salih from Jabir from Abu Ja‘far (عليه‌السلام) from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

These two narrations, despite their state—especially the second one (1) due to the weakness of its chain—cannot be relied upon because they contradict the apparent meaning of the Book, which explicitly states the infallibility (عصمة) of the angels and their being free from sin and error.

Al-Tha‘labi narrated in al-‘Ara’is from Ibn ‘Abbas and others, summarized as follows: "Idris was walking one day and was struck by the heat of the sun. He said: 'I walked in the sun for one day and was pained; so what of the one who carries it for a journey of five hundred years in a single day! O Allah, lighten its weight for him and take its heat away from him.' Allah answered him, and the angel who carries it felt that. He asked Allah about it, and He informed him of Idris's prayer and its acceptance. The angel asked Him, the Exalted, to bring him and Idris together and make a friendship between them, and He permitted him.

Idris used to ask him, and among what he asked was: 'You have informed me that you are the most honored of angels to the Angel of Death and the most influential with him; so intercede for me with him to delay my term (أجلي) so that I may increase in gratitude and worship.' The angel said: 'Allah does not delay a soul when its term has come.' He said: 'Yes, but it would be more pleasing to my soul.' The angel said: 'I shall speak to him for you, and whatever he is able to do for any of the children of Adam, he will do for you.'

Then the angel carried him on his wing and raised him to the heaven, placing him at the rising of the sun. Then he came to the Angel of Death and mentioned Idris's need and interceded for him. The Angel of Death said: 'That is not up to me, but if you wish, I can inform him of his term.' He said: 'Yes.' So he looked into his register and informed him of his name and said: 'I do not see that he will ever die, for I find that he is to die at the rising of the sun!' The angel said: 'I have come to you having left him there.' He said to him: 'Go, for I do not think you will find him except dead, for by Allah, nothing remains of his term.' The angel returned to him and found him dead."

This was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Abi Shaybah and Ibn Abi Hatim from Ibn ‘Abbas from Ka‘b, except that it mentions that the one who descended to Idris was the angel who used to raise his deeds; deeds equivalent to the deeds of all the people of the earth in his time were being raised for him, which pleased the angel, so he asked Allah to descend to him, and He permitted him, so he accompanied him, etc. Ibn Abi Hatim narrated this from another route from Ibn Abbas, which says: "Idris died between the wings of the angel."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn al-Mundhir recorded from ‘Umar, the client of Ghafrah, tracing it to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): "For Idris alone, deeds equivalent to the deeds of all the people of the earth were being raised. This pleased the Angel of Death, so he sought permission from Allah to descend to the earth and accompany him. He permitted him, so he descended and accompanied him. They used to travel through the earth and worship Allah. Idris was amazed by the worship he saw from his companion, without laziness or fatigue. He asked him about it and persisted in the questioning until the Angel of Death identified himself and mentioned the story of his descent and companionship.

When Idris recognized him, he asked him for three needs: that he take his soul for an hour then return it to him—so he sought permission from Allah and did so; that he raise him to the heaven and show him the Fire—so he sought permission and did so; and that he show him Paradise—so he sought permission and did so. He entered Paradise, ate of its fruits, and drank of its water. The Angel of Death said to him: 'Come out, O Prophet of Allah, for you have attained your need.' He refused to come out and clung to a tree there.

He argued with the Angel of Death, saying: 'Allah said: "Every soul shall taste death," and I have tasted it. And He said: "There is none of you but will come to it [the Fire]," and I have come to it. And He said: "And they shall not be expelled from it [Paradise]," and I am not coming out of Paradise after entering it.' Allah revealed to the Angel of Death: 'My servant has argued with you, so leave him and do not interfere with him.' So he remained in Paradise."

It was narrated in al-‘Ara’is from Wahb, and at its end: "He is alive there; sometimes he worships Allah in the fourth heaven, and sometimes he enjoys bliss in Paradise."

In Mustadrak al-Hakim, from Samurah: "Idris was white, tall, broad-chested, with little body hair and much hair on his head. One of his eyes was larger than the other, and there was a white spot on his chest that was not leprosy. When Allah saw what He saw from the people of the earth of their injustice and transgression in Allah's affair, He raised him to the sixth heaven; he is where He says: 'And We raised him to a station sublime.'"

I say: The discerning critic has no doubt that these narrations are Isra’iliyyat (إسرائيليات) — narrations of Judeo-Christian origin that have been manipulated by fabricators — and they are refuted by scientific standards and the established principles of the religion.

3 — He (عليه‌السلام) is also called Hermes (هرمس). Al-Qifti said in the book Ikhbar al-‘Ulama’ bi-Akhbar al-Hukama’, in the biography of Idris: "The sages have differed regarding his birthplace, his upbringing, and from whom he took knowledge before prophethood. One group said: He was born in Egypt and they called him Hermes of the Hermeses (هرمس الهرامسة), and his birthplace was in Memphis (منف). They said: He is Ermis in Greek, and it was Arabized as Hermes, and the meaning of Ermis is Mercury (عطارد). Others said: His name in Greek is Tarmis, and he is Khunukh (Enoch) among the Hebrews, Arabized as Akhnukh, and Allah, Mighty and Majestic, named him Idris in His clear Arabic Book.

These people said: His teacher's name was Ghawthadhimun, and it is said Aghathadhimun the Egyptian. They did not mention who this man was, except that they said he was one of the Greek and Egyptian prophets. They also called him the second Orion, and Idris is the third Orion to them. The interpretation of Ghawthadhimun is 'the fortunate one.' They said: Hermes left Egypt and traversed the whole earth, then returned to it, and Allah raised him to Himself there, after eighty-two years of his life.

Another group said: Idris was born in Babylon and grew up there, and in the beginning of his life, he took the knowledge of Seth (شيث), the son of Adam, who was his great-great-grandfather, because Idris is the son of Jared, son of Mahalalel, son of Cainan, son of Enos, son of Seth. Al-Shahrastani said: Aghathadhimun is Seth.

When Idris grew up, Allah gave him prophethood, so he forbade the corrupt among the children of Adam from opposing the law (شريعة) of Adam and Seth. A few of them obeyed him while the majority opposed him. He intended to migrate from them and ordered those who obeyed him to do so. Migration from their homelands was difficult for them, so they said to him: 'Where shall we find the like of Babylon if we migrate?' Babylon in Syriac means 'the river,' and it is as if they meant the Tigris and Euphrates. He said: 'If we migrate for Allah, He will provide us with another.'

So he went out and they went out, and they traveled until they reached this region which was named Babylion. They saw the Nile and saw a valley empty of inhabitants. Idris stood by the Nile, glorified Allah, and said to his group: 'Babylion.' There is disagreement over its interpretation; it is said: 'a great river,' and it is said: 'a river like your river,' and it is said: 'a blessed river.' It is said that yun in Syriac is like the superlative form in Arabic, and its meaning was 'a greater river.' So the region was named Babylion by all nations, and all groups of nations agree on that except the Arabs, for they call it the region of Egypt (مصر) after Misraim son of Ham who settled in it after the Flood. Allah knows best about all that.

Idris and those with him stayed in Egypt, calling the creation to bidding the right and forbidding the wrong and obedience to Allah, Mighty and Majestic. In his days, people spoke seventy-two languages, and Allah, Mighty and Majestic, taught him their speech so he could teach every group in its own language. He designed for them the urbanization of cities (تمدين المدن), and seekers of knowledge from every city gathered to him, so he taught them civil politics (السياسة المدنية) and established its rules for them. Every group of nations built cities in its land, and the number of cities established in his time was one hundred and eighty-eight cities, the smallest of which was Edessa (الرها), and he taught them the sciences.

He was the first to extract wisdom (الحكمة) and the science of the stars (علم النجوم), for Allah, Mighty and Majestic, made him understand the secret of the celestial sphere (الفلك), its composition, and the points of conjunction of the planets therein, and He made him understand the number of years and calculation (الحساب). Had it not been for that, human thoughts through their investigation would not have reached that.

He established laws (سننا) for the nations in every region, each law suiting its people. He divided the earth into four quarters and appointed over each quarter a king to manage the affairs of the inhabited part of that quarter. He instructed each king to oblige the people of each quarter with a law, some of which I shall mention. The names of the four kings who ruled were: first, Ilaos, meaning 'the merciful'; second, Aus; third, Asqulabiyus; and fourth, Aus Amun, and it is said Ilaos Amun, and it is said Yasilukhas, who is Amun the King." Here ends the relevant portion.

These are narrations and reports that trace back to pre-history and cannot be fully relied upon. However, the survival of his living memory among philosophers and people of knowledge generation after generation, their glorification of him, their respect for his presence, and their attribution of the foundations of science to him, reveals that he is among the oldest of the leaders of knowledge who led the human world to the arena of demonstrative thought (التفكر الاستدلالي) and profound investigation into divine knowledge (المعارف الإلهية), or rather, he is the first of them (عليه‌السلام).

Ayahs

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِم مِّنَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ مِن ذُرِّيَّةِ ءَادَمَ وَمِمَّنْ حَمَلْنَا مَعَ نُوحٍ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّةِ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَإِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ وَمِمَّنْ هَدَيْنَا وَٱجْتَبَيْنَآ ۚ إِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ خَرُّوا۟ سُجَّدًا وَبُكِيًّا ۩ (٥٨)
They are the ones whom Allah has blessed from among the prophets of Adam’s progeny, and from [the progeny of] those We carried with Noah, and from among the progeny of Abraham and Israel, and from among those that We guided and chose. When the signs of the All-beneficent were recited to them, they would fall down weeping in prostration. (58)
۞ فَخَلَفَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ خَلْفٌ أَضَاعُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَٱتَّبَعُوا۟ ٱلشَّهَوَٰتِ ۖ فَسَوْفَ يَلْقَوْنَ غَيًّا (٥٩)
But they were succeeded by an evil posterity who neglected the prayer, and followed [their base] appetites. So they will soon encounter [the reward of] perversity, (59)
إِلَّا مَن تَابَ وَءَامَنَ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ يَدْخُلُونَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ وَلَا يُظْلَمُونَ شَيْـًٔا (٦٠)
barring those who repent, believe, and act righteously. Such will enter paradise, and they will not be wronged in the least. (60)
جَنَّـٰتِ عَدْنٍ ٱلَّتِى وَعَدَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ عِبَادَهُۥ بِٱلْغَيْبِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ وَعْدُهُۥ مَأْتِيًّا (٦١)
Gardens of Eden promised by the All-beneficent to His servants, [while they were still] unseen. Indeed His promise is bound to come to pass. (61)
لَّا يَسْمَعُونَ فِيهَا لَغْوًا إِلَّا سَلَـٰمًا ۖ وَلَهُمْ رِزْقُهُمْ فِيهَا بُكْرَةً وَعَشِيًّا (٦٢)
Therein they will not hear vain talk, but only ‘Peace!’ Therein they will have their provision morning and evening. (62)
تِلْكَ ٱلْجَنَّةُ ٱلَّتِى نُورِثُ مِنْ عِبَادِنَا مَن كَانَ تَقِيًّا (٦٣)
This is the paradise We will give as inheritance to those of Our servants who are Godwary. (63)

(Commentary)

It has already been mentioned in the discussion regarding the purpose of the Surah that what is gleaned from its context is an exposition that worshiping Him, the Exalted—which is the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts—is the religion of the people of felicity and rectitude among the prophets and the saints (الأولياء). Furthermore, it clarifies that deviating from their path by neglecting prayer and following lusts is an adherence to the path of error (الغي), except for those who repent, believe, and perform righteous deeds.

Thus, the verses, especially the first three, contain the core purpose of the Surah. The author has presented this in the form of a deduction from the stories narrated in the preceding verses. This is one of the features that distinguishes this Surah from other long Surahs of the Quran; in other Surahs, the objectives are usually alluded to through the "excellence of the opening" (براعة الاستهلال) at the beginning and the "beauty of the conclusion" (حسن الختام) at the end, rather than in the middle.

His saying, the Exalted: أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنَ النَّبِيِّينَ — Those are the ones whom Allah has blessed from among the prophets (19:58) etc. The demonstration in His saying "Those" (أُولئِكَ) refers to those mentioned before this verse in the Surah, namely: Zakariyya, Yahya, Maryam, ‘Isa, Ibrahim, Ishaq, Ya‘qub, Musa, Harun, Isma‘il, and Idris (عليه السلام).

It has already been alluded to from the context of the Surah's verses that the stories cited therein are examples, and that this verse and the two following it are a conclusion extracted from them. Consequently, His saying "Those" (أُولئِكَ) refers specifically to the subjects of the stories as the subject (مبتدأ), while His saying "whom Allah has blessed" (الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ) is its description (صفة), and His saying "when they are recited to them" (إِذا تُتْلى عَلَيْهِمْ) etc., is its predicate (خبر). This is what contemplation (التدبر) on the context leads to. If one were to take "whom Allah has blessed" as the predicate for "Those," then "when they are recited to them" would be a second predicate after the first, but this would not harmonize as perfectly with the purpose of the Surah.

Allah, the Glorified, has informed us that He has blessed them and spoke of them in absolute terms. This indicates that the divine blessing (النعمة) has enveloped them without any trace of vengeance (نقمة), and this is the very meaning of felicity (السعادة) — the state of ultimate well-being and divine favor. For felicity is nothing other than blessing without vengeance. These are the people of felicity and success in the fullest sense of the word. The Exalted has informed us that they are the people of the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah—whose wayfarer is protected from wrath and straying, as He said: اهْدِنَا الصِّراطَ الْمُسْتَقِيمَ صِراطَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ غَيْرِ الْمَغْضُوبِ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا الضَّالِّينَ — Guide us on the straight path, the path of those whom You have blessed — such as have not incurred Your wrath, nor are astray (1:6-7). They are in security and guidance due to His saying: الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَلَمْ يَلْبِسُوا إِيمانَهُمْ بِظُلْمٍ أُولئِكَ لَهُمُ الْأَمْنُ وَهُمْ مُهْتَدُونَ — Those who have faith and do not taint their faith with wrongdoing — for such there shall be safety, and they are the [rightly] guided (6:82). Thus, the companions of the straight path, who are protected from wrath and straying and have not tainted their faith with wrongdoing, are secure from every danger that threatens a human being. They are felicitous in their conduct of the path of life they have trodden, and the path they have trodden is the path of felicity.

His saying "from among the prophets" (مِنَ النَّبِيِّينَ): the word "from" (من) here denotes partitivity (التبعيض), and its counterpart is His subsequent saying: "and from those whom We guided and chose" (وَمِمَّنْ هَدَيْنا وَاجْتَبَيْنا), as will be clarified. Some exegetes have allowed for "from" to be explanatory (بيانية), but you are well aware that this does not harmonize with "Those" (أُولئِكَ) referring to those mentioned previously, because "the prophets" is a more general term. Unless, of course, it refers to them insofar as they are examples of the people of felicity, meaning: those mentioned and their likes whom Allah has blessed are the prophets and those whom We have guided and chosen.

His saying "from the progeny of Adam" (مِنْ ذُرِّيَّةِ آدَمَ) is in the sense of a description for the prophets, and "from" denotes partitivity; i.e., from the prophets who are a portion of the progeny of Adam. It is not an explanation for "the prophets," as that would disrupt the meaning.

His saying "and from those We carried with Noah" (وَمِمَّنْ حَمَلْنا مَعَ نُوحٍ) is coordinated (معطوف) with His saying "from the progeny of Adam." What is meant by them are those carried in the ark of Noah (عليه السلام) and their progeny, whom Allah blessed. They are from the progeny of Noah because of the saying of the Exalted: وَجَعَلْنا ذُرِّيَّتَهُ هُمُ الْباقِينَ — and made his descendants the survivors (37:77).

His saying "and from the progeny of Abraham and Israel" (وَمِنْ ذُرِّيَّةِ إِبْراهِيمَ وَإِسْرائِيلَ) is coordinated, like the previous one, with His saying "from among the prophets."

Allah the Exalted has divided those He blessed among the prophets into these four groups: the progeny of Adam, those He carried with Noah, the progeny of Abraham, and the progeny of Israel. Mentioning each preceding group would have sufficed for the subsequent one, since the progeny of Israel are from the progeny of Abraham, and all are from those carried with Noah, and all are from the progeny of Adam (عليه السلام).

Perhaps the reason for this is to point toward the descent of the blessing of felicity and the benediction of prophethood upon the human species time after time. This has been mentioned in the Noble Quran in four places for four groups: First, for the generality of the Children of Adam, where He said: قُلْنَا اهْبِطُوا مِنْها جَمِيعاً فَإِمَّا يَأْتِيَنَّكُمْ مِنِّي هُدىً فَمَنْ تَبِعَ هُدايَ فَلا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَكَذَّبُوا بِآياتِنا أُولئِكَ أَصْحابُ النَّارِ هُمْ فِيها خالِدُونَ — We said, ‘Get down from it, all together! Yet, should any guidance come to you from Me, those who follow My guidance shall have no fear, nor shall they grieve. As for those who are faithless and deny Our signs, they shall be the inmates of the Fire and they shall remain in it [forever] (2:38-39).

Second, what is in His saying, the Exalted: قِيلَ يا نُوحُ اهْبِطْ بِسَلامٍ مِنَّا وَبَرَكاتٍ عَلَيْكَ وَعَلى أُمَمٍ مِمَّنْ مَعَكَ وَأُمَمٌ سَنُمَتِّعُهمْ ثُمَّ يَمَسُّهُمْ مِنَّا عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ — It was said, ‘O Noah, descend with peace from Us, and blessings on you and on the nations who are with you. And there will be nations whom We shall provide for, then a painful punishment from Us will visit them’ (11:48). Third, what is in His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنا نُوحاً وَإِبْراهِيمَ وَجَعَلْنا فِي ذُرِّيَّتِهِمَا النُّبُوَّةَ وَالْكِتابَ فَمِنْهُمْ مُهْتَدٍ وَكَثِيرٌ مِنْهُمْ فاسِقُونَ — Certainly We sent Noah and Abraham and We ordained prophethood and the Book among their descendants. Some of them are [rightly] guided, and many of them are transgressors (57:26). Fourth, what is in His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنا بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ الْكِتابَ وَالْحُكْمَ وَالنُّبُوَّةَ وَرَزَقْناهُمْ مِنَ الطَّيِّباتِ وَفَضَّلْناهُمْ عَلَى الْعالَمِينَ — Certainly We gave the Children of Israel the Book, judgement and prophethood and We provided them with all the good things and We gave them an advantage over all the nations (45:16).

These are four promises regarding the singling out of the human species with the blessing of prophethood and the gift of felicity. They are alluded to in the verse under discussion by His saying: "from among the prophets from the progeny of Adam, and from those We carried with Noah, and from the progeny of Abraham and Israel." In the preceding stories, individuals from each of these four lineages were mentioned: such as Idris from the progeny of Adam; Abraham from the progeny of those carried with Noah; Ishaq and Ya‘qub from the progeny of Abraham; and Zakariyya, Yahya, ‘Isa, Musa, Harun, and Isma‘il—according to what we have inferred—from the progeny of Israel.

His saying "and from those We guided and chose" (وَمِمَّنْ هَدَيْنا وَاجْتَبَيْنا) is coordinated with His saying "from among the prophets." These are others besides the prophets from among those whom Allah has blessed, for this blessing is not exclusive to the prophets nor restricted to them, as evidenced by the saying of the Exalted: وَمَنْ يُطِعِ اللهَ وَالرَّسُولَ فَأُولئِكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنَ النَّبِيِّينَ وَالصِّدِّيقِينَ وَالشُّهَداءِ وَالصَّالِحِينَ وَحَسُنَ أُولئِكَ رَفِيقاً — Whoever obeys Allah and the Apostle — they are with those whom Allah has blessed, including the prophets and the truthful, the martyrs and the righteous, and excellent companions are they! (4:69). Allah, the Glorified, mentioned among those whose story He narrated Maryam (عليه السلام), paying great attention to her when He said: "And mention Maryam in the Book." She was not among the prophets. Therefore, what is meant by His saying "and from those We guided and chose" must necessarily be those other than the prophets, such as the truthful (الصديقين), the martyrs (الشهداء), and the righteous (الصالحين). Maryam was among the truthful, as per the saying of the Exalted: مَا الْمَسِيحُ ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ إِلَّا رَسُولٌ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ الرُّسُلُ وَأُمُّهُ صِدِّيقَةٌ — The Messiah, son of Mary, is but an apostle. Certainly [other] apostles have passed before him, and his mother was a truthful woman (5:75).

From what has preceded regarding the requirements of the context, the invalidity of the view of those who made "and from those We guided and chose" coordinated with "from among the prophets" while taking "from" as explanatory becomes clear. Some have also objected to this by stating that the apparent meaning of coordination (العطف) implies a distinction (المغايرة); thus, one would need to say: what is meant are those for whom We combined prophethood, guidance, and selection for honor—which is contrary to the apparent meaning. However, this objection is countered by the fact that the apparent meaning of coordination does not require a distinction in external identity (مصداقاً), but rather a distinction in general, even if only in terms of description and explanation.

Similar to this is the view of those who said it is coordinated with His saying "from the progeny of Adam" with "from" denoting partitivity; the reason for its invalidity has already been clarified.

Also similar is the view of those who said that His saying "and from those We guided" is a new sentence (استئناف) without coordination. According to them, the speech ended at "Israel," then He began anew, saying: "And from those We guided and chose from the nations are a people who, when the signs of the All-Beneficent are recited to them, fall down prostrating and weeping." Thus, the subject (مبتدأ) was omitted because the speech points to it. This view is attributed to the exegete Abu Muslim.

The flaw in this is that it is an assumption without evidence. Furthermore, it corrupts the purpose as witnessed by the context, for the purpose is to explain the way of those blessed servants—that they were submissive and humble before Allah—and that their successors turned away from their path, neglected prayer, and followed lusts. This cannot be achieved unless His saying "when they are recited to them" etc., is the predicate for His saying "Those are the ones whom Allah has blessed." Taking His saying "and from those We guided" to the end of the verse as a new sentence severed from what preceded it corrupts the aforementioned purpose from the very root.

His saying: إِذا تُتْلى عَلَيْهِمْ آياتُ الرَّحْمنِ خَرُّوا سُجَّداً وَبُكِيًّا — when the signs of the All-beneficent were recited to them, they would fall down in prostration and weeping (19:58). The word "prostrating" (سجداً) is the plural of "prostrator" (ساجد), and "weeping" (بكيّاً) is on the pattern of fu‘ūl, the plural of "weeper" (باكي). The sentence is the predicate for "those" at the beginning of the verse. It is possible that falling down in prostration and weeping is a metaphor for perfect submission (خضوع) and humility (خشوع); for prostration is the manifestation of perfect submission, and weeping is the manifestation of perfect humility. In this light, it is more appropriate that "the signs" (الآيات) and their "recitation" refer to the mention of anything that narrates an affair of His affairs, the Exalted.

As for the claim that the recitation of the signs refers to reading the heavenly books generally, or specifically those containing the punishment of the disbelievers and criminals; or that prostration refers to the ritual prayer (الصلاة) or the prostration of recitation (سجدة التلاوة); or that weeping refers to crying upon hearing or reciting the verses—these are as you see [unfounded].

The meaning of the verse—and Allah knows best—is: those blessed ones, some of whom are prophets from the progeny of Adam and from those We carried with Noah and from the progeny of Abraham and Israel, and some of whom are the people of guidance and selection, are submissive to the All-Beneficent and humble when He is mentioned to them and His signs are recited before them.

He did not say: "they used to (كانوا) when they are recited to them" etc., because the concern in this context is focused on explaining the state of the species without regard to past or future time, but rather by dividing it into a righteous predecessor (سلف صالح), an evil successor (خلف طالح), and a third group that repented, believed, and performed righteous deeds. This is evident.

His saying, the Exalted: فَخَلَفَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ خَلْفٌ أَضاعُوا الصَّلاةَ وَاتَّبَعُوا الشَّهَواتِ فَسَوْفَ يَلْقَوْنَ غَيًّا — But they were succeeded by a generations who neglected the prayer and followed [their] appetites. So they will soon encounter leeway (19:59). They said: the word khalf (الخلف) with a sukūn on the lām refers to an evil replacement, while with a fathah on the lām it is its opposite, though they are occasionally used interchangeably. The "loss of a thing" (ضياع الشيء) is its corruption or its being missing due to what should have been its safeguard. It is said: "he lost the wealth" (أضاع المال) if he corrupted it through poor management or removed it from his hand by spending it on what it should not be spent on. Error (الغي) is the opposite of rectitude (الرشد) — the attainment of reality. It is close in meaning to straying (الضلال), which is the opposite of guidance (الهدى) — the treading of the path that leads to the intended goal.

Thus, His saying "But they were succeeded by a generation" etc., means that in the place of those whom Allah blessed—whose way was submission and humility to Allah the Exalted by turning to Him in worship—there arose an evil people who neglected what they had received from them of prayer and the devotional turning toward Allah, the Glorified, by being negligent of it and turning away from it. They followed lusts that diverted them from striving for Allah and turning toward Him.

From here it becomes clear that what is meant by "neglecting the prayer" (إضاعة الصلاة) is corrupting it through negligence and disdain, until it ends in things like playing with it, altering it, or abandoning it after having accepted it. Therefore, what has been said—that neglecting the prayer means abandoning it—is not sound; for the initial abandonment of a thing is not called "neglecting" (إضاعة) it. The concern in the verse is that the divine religion was transferred from those righteous predecessors to these evil successors, but they did not fulfill the succession well and neglected what they inherited of the prayer, which is the sole pillar of servitude (العبودية), and followed the lusts that divert from the Truth.

His saying "So they will soon encounter leeway" (فَسَوْفَ يَلْقَوْنَ غَيًّا) means the recompense of their error, as has been said. It is like His saying: وَمَنْ يَفْعَلْ ذلِكَ يَلْقَ أَثاماً — and whoever does that shall encounter the reward of sin (25:68).

It is possible that what is meant is the error (الغي) itself, by considering error as the end of the path they tread—which is the path of neglecting prayer and following lusts.
