Since they are treading a path whose end is error, they will encounter it when they complete it, either by their error being unveiled to them on the Day of Resurrection when realities are disclosed, or by the entrenchment of error in their hearts and their becoming among the friends of Satan, as He said: إِنَّ عِبادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطانٌ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغاوِينَ — As for My servants, you shall have no authority over them, except such of the perverse as follow you (16:42). In any case, it is a subtle metaphor (استعارة بالكناية).

His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا مَنْ تابَ وَآمَنَ وَعَمِلَ صالِحاً فَأُولئِكَ يَدْخُلُونَ الْجَنَّةَ وَلا يُظْلَمُونَ شَيْئاً — excepting those who repent, believe, and act righteously. Such will enter paradise, and they will not be wronged in the least (19:60). This is an exception from the previous verse. These who return to Allah the Glorified are joined with those whom Allah has blessed; they are with them but not [originally] of them, as the Exalted said: وَمَنْ يُطِعِ اللهَ وَالرَّسُولَ فَأُولئِكَ مَعَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللهُ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنَ النَّبِيِّينَ وَالصِّدِّيقِينَ وَالشُّهَداءِ وَالصَّالِحِينَ وَحَسُنَ أُولئِكَ رَفِيقاً — Whoever obeys Allah and the Apostle — they are with those whom Allah has blessed, including the prophets and the truthful, the martyrs and the righteous, and excellent companions are they! (4:69).

His saying "Such will enter paradise" (فَأُولئِكَ يَدْخُلُونَ الْجَنَّةَ) is a case of placing the effect in the position of the cause. The original meaning is: "Such will be paid their reward in full." The evidence for this is His subsequent saying: "and they will not be wronged in the least," for this is a consequence of the full payment of reward, not a consequence of entering Paradise.

His saying, the Exalted: جَنَّاتِ عَدْنٍ الَّتِي وَعَدَ الرَّحْمنُ عِبادَهُ بِالْغَيْبِ إِنَّهُ كانَ وَعْدُهُ مَأْتِيًّا — the gardens of Eden, which the All-beneficent has promised His servants in the Unseen. Indeed His promise is bound to come (19:61). ‘Adn (العدن) means residence; naming it so is an allusion to its eternity for those who enter it. The "promise in the Unseen" (الوعد بالغيب) is the promise of that which is not within the perception of the one promised. The promise being "bound to come" (مأتياً) means it will not fail. It is said in al-Majma‘: the passive participle (المفعول) here is in the sense of the active participle (الفاعل), because what you have come to has come to you, and what has come to you, you have come to. It is said: "I have come to fifty years" and "fifty years have come upon me." It is also said: the promised thing is Paradise, and the believers come to Paradise. End quote.

His saying, the Exalted: لا يَسْمَعُونَ فِيها لَغْواً إِلَّا سَلاماً وَلَهُمْ رِزْقُهُمْ فِيها بُكْرَةً وَعَشِيًّا — They will not hear therein any vain talk, but only ‘Peace!’ And therein they will have their provision morning and evening (19:62). Not hearing vain talk (اللغو) is among the most specific attributes of Paradise. Allah, the Glorified, has mentioned it and bestowed it as a favor in several places in His speech, and we shall detail the discussion on it, Allah willing, in a suitable place. The exception of "Peace" (السلام) from it is a disconnected exception (استثناء منفصل). Peace is close in meaning to security (الأمن)—the difference between them has already been mentioned—for your saying "You are in security from me" means you will not encounter from me what displeases you, while your saying "Peace from me be upon you" means everything you encounter from me will not displease you. They only hear peace from the angels and from their companions in Paradise. The Exalted said, narrating from the angels: سَلامٌ عَلَيْكُمْ طِبْتُمْ — ‘Peace be to you! You are welcome!’ (39:73), and He said: فَسَلامٌ لَكَ مِنْ أَصْحابِ الْيَمِينِ — then [he will be greeted with], ‘Peace be to you, from the People of the Right!’ (56:91).

His saying "And therein they will have their provision morning and evening" (وَلَهُمْ رِزْقُهُمْ فِيها بُكْرَةً وَعَشِيًّا): it appears that the arrival of provision morning and evening is a metaphor for its continuity without interruption.

His saying, the Exalted: تِلْكَ الْجَنَّةُ الَّتِي نُورِثُ مِنْ عِبادِنا مَنْ كانَ تَقِيًّا — This is the paradise We will give as inheritance to those of Our servants who are God-conscious (19:63). Inheritance (الإرث والوراثة) is the transfer of property or its like from one person to another after the first has left it due to death, departure, or the like. Since Paradise was offered to every human being according to the divine promise conditioned upon faith and righteous deeds, the exclusive assignment of it to the God-conscious (المتقين) — those who possess godwariness and are mindful of Allah—after others were deprived of it due to neglecting prayer and following lusts, is the inheritance of the God-conscious. Similar to this motif is what is in His saying, the Exalted: أَنَّ الْأَرْضَ يَرِثُها عِبادِيَ الصَّالِحُونَ — that My righteous servants shall inherit the earth (21:105), and His saying: وَقالُوا الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ الَّذِي صَدَقَنا وَعْدَهُ وَأَوْرَثَنَا الْأَرْضَ نَتَبَوَّأُ مِنَ الْجَنَّةِ حَيْثُ نَشاءُ فَنِعْمَ أَجْرُ الْعامِلِينَ — They will say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, who has fulfilled His promise to us and made us inherit the earth, that we may settle in paradise wherever we may wish!’ How excellent is the reward of those who act! (39:74). The verse—as you see—combines inheritance and reward.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَمِمَّنْ هَدَيْنا وَاجْتَبَيْنا — and from those We guided and chose (19:58), it is narrated from Imam ‘Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him) that he said: نحن عنينا بها — "We are the ones intended by it."

I say: And from Manaqib of Ibn Shahr Ashub, from him (peace be upon him), the same is narrated. The meaning of the hadith has become clear from what we provided in the interpretation of the verse; for what is meant by the phrase are the people of guidance and selection other than the prophets, and they (peace be upon them) are among them, just as Allah the Glorified mentioned Maryam as being among them though she was not a prophet.

The author of Ruh al-Ma‘ani said: "Some of the Imamiyyah narrated from ‘Ali ibn al-Husayn (may Allah be pleased with them both) that he said: 'We are the ones intended by these people.' It is not hidden that this is very much against the apparent meaning, and the state of the Imamiyyah's narrations is not hidden from those possessed of discernment." End quote. His error is evident from what has preceded. What led him into this was his taking the saying of the Exalted "and from those We guided and chose" as coordinated with His saying "from the progeny of Adam," and His saying "from among the prophets" as an explanation for His saying "Those are the ones" etc. Thus, "those whom Allah has blessed" became restricted to the prophets, so he was forced to say that the verse does not include anyone other than the prophets, even though he sees that Allah mentioned among those He mentioned Maryam daughter of ‘Imran, and she was not a prophet.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ahmad, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Hibban, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in Shu‘ab al-Iman narrated from Abu Sa‘id al-Khudri who said: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) reciting this verse: فَخَلَفَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ خَلْفٌ — But they were succeeded by a generation—and he said: يكون خلف من بعد ستين سنة أضاعوا الصلاة ـ واتبعوا الشهوات فسوف يلقون غيا ، ثم يكون خلف يقرءون القرآن لا يعدو تراقيهم ، ويقرأ القرآن ثلاثة : مؤمن ومنافق وفاجر — 'There will be a generation after sixty years who will neglect the prayer and follow lusts, so they will soon encounter error. Then there will be a generation who recite the Quran but it does not go beyond their collarbones. And three types recite the Quran: a believer, a hypocrite, and a profligate.'"

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: أَضاعُوا الصَّلاةَ — they neglected the prayer: "It is said: they neglected it by delaying it from its proper times without abandoning it entirely—and this is what is narrated from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him)."

I say: It is narrated in al-Kafi with its chain to Dawud ibn Farqad from him (peace be upon him) to the same effect. This has also been narrated through Sunni channels from Ibn Mas‘ud and a number of the Successors (التابعين).

From Jawami‘ al-Jami‘ and in Ruh al-Ma‘ani: regarding His saying: وَاتَّبَعُوا الشَّهَواتِ — and followed [their] appetites, from Imam ‘Ali (peace be upon him): من بنى الشديد وركب المنظور ولبس المشهور — "Whoever builds the sturdy, rides the conspicuous, and wears the famous."

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ibn Mardawayh narrated through the path of Nahshal from al-Dahhak from Ibn ‘Abbas from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) who said: الغي واد في جهنم — "Error (الغي) is a valley in Hell."

I say: In other narrations, it is said that al-Ghayy and al-Atham are two rivers in Hell. This, provided the hadith is authentic, is not a different interpretation as most exegetes have presumed, but rather an explanation of what the error will lead to in terms of recompense. It is similar to what has been narrated that al-Wayl is a well in Hell and that Tuba is a tree in Paradise, among other such narrations.

Ayahs

وَمَا نَتَنَزَّلُ إِلَّا بِأَمْرِ رَبِّكَ ۖ لَهُۥ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِينَا وَمَا خَلْفَنَا وَمَا بَيْنَ ذَٰلِكَ ۚ وَمَا كَانَ رَبُّكَ نَسِيًّا (٦٤)
[O Gabriel, tell the Prophet,] ‘We do not descend except by the command of your Lord. To Him belongs whatever is before us and whatever is behind us and whatever is in between that, and your Lord does not forget (64)
رَّبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا فَٱعْبُدْهُ وَٱصْطَبِرْ لِعِبَـٰدَتِهِۦ ۚ هَلْ تَعْلَمُ لَهُۥ سَمِيًّا (٦٥)
—the Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them. So worship Him and be steadfast in His worship. Do you know anyone who could be His namesake?’ (65)

(Commentary)

These two verses are parenthetical (معترضتان) within the verses of the Surah, and their context (سياق) testifies that they are the speech of the Angel of Revelation through a Quranic revelation from Allah, the Glorified, for the composition is undoubtedly Quranic. This is supported by what has been narrated through various chains from the Sunni sources, and also narrated in Majma‘ al-Bayan from Ibn Abbas: that the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله found the descent of Jibril to be slow, so he asked him about that. Jibril then answered him with a revelation from Allah, the Exalted: وَمَا نَتَنَزَّلُ إِلَّا بِأَمْرِ رَبِّكَ "We do not descend except by the command of your Lord..." until the end of the two verses.

A group of commentators has exerted great effort to explain the connection of these two verses to the preceding ones. Some said the implied meaning is: "This [is the case], and Jibril said: 'We do not descend except by the command of your Lord...'" Others said they are connected to the speech of Jibril to Maryam mentioned previously: إِنَّمَا أَنَا رَسُولُ رَبِّكِ لِأَهَبَ لَكِ غُلَامًا زَكِيًّا "I am only a messenger of your Lord that I may give you a pure son." A group mentioned that His saying: وَمَا نَتَنَزَّلُ إِلَّا بِأَمْرِ رَبِّكَ until the end of the verse is the speech of the God-conscious (المتقين) — those who possess godwariness (تقوى), the protective shield of the soul against sin and divine displeasure — when they enter Paradise; thus the implied meaning is: "And the God-conscious said: 'We do not descend into Paradise except by the command of your Lord...'" Other views have also been mentioned.

All of these are views of apparent absurdity (سخافة) which the context rejects and the eloquent composition (النظم البليغ) does not accept; there is no need to occupy oneself with explaining the facets of their invalidity. Another perspective on the connection will follow in the discussion of the second verse.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا نَتَنَزَّلُ إِلَّا بِأَمْرِ رَبِّكَ "We do not descend except by the command of your Lord" until the end of the verse. Descent (التنزل) is to come down with deliberation and composure, for tanazzala (تنزل) is the reflexive form of nazzala (نزل); it is said: "He sent it down (نزله), so it descended (فتنزل)." The negation and exception signify restriction (الحصر), meaning the angels do not descend except by a command from Allah, as He said: لَا يَعْصُونَ اللَّهَ مَا أَمَرَهُمْ وَيَفْعَلُونَ مَا يُؤْمَرُونَ "They do not disobey Allah in what He has commanded them, and they carry out what they are commanded." (At-Tahrim: 6).

And His saying: لَهُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِينَا وَمَا خَلْفَنَا وَمَا بَيْنَ ذَلِكَ "To Him belongs whatever is before us and whatever is behind us and whatever is in between." It is said: "Such a thing is in front of him (قدامه), ahead of him (أمامه), and between his hands (بين يديه)," and the meaning is the same, except that our saying "between his hands" is only used for what is in close proximity to him, such that he oversees it and has a type of disposal (تصرف) and authority (تسلط) over it. Thus, the apparent meaning of His saying: مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِينَا "whatever is before us" is that which we oversee, which is exposed to us and witnessed by us. The apparent meaning of His saying: وَمَا خَلْفَنَا "whatever is behind us," by contrast, is that which is absent from us and hidden from us.

Accordingly, if "whatever is before us and whatever is behind us and whatever is in between" were intended to mean space (المكان), it would encompass some of the space in front of them, the space they are currently in, and all the space behind them, but it would not encompass every place. Similarly, if it were intended to mean time (الزمان), it would encompass all of the past, the present, and only the near future. However, the context of His saying: لَهُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِينَا وَمَا خَلْفَنَا وَمَا بَيْنَ ذَلِكَ calls for all-encompassingness (الإحاطة) and does not suit partiality (التبعيض).

Therefore, the correct view is to interpret "whatever is before us" as the actions and effects branching out from their existence, which they are engaged in and have authority over; to interpret "whatever is behind us" as the causes of their existence that preceded them and were realized before them; and to interpret "whatever is in between" as their very existence (وجودهم) itself. This is among the most innovative and subtle forms of expression. By this, the divine encompassment of them is completed from every direction, as the meaning returns to the fact that Allah, the Exalted, is the Owner of our existence and everything our existence relates to, before and after.

Opinions have differed regarding the interpretation of this sentence. It was said: what is intended by "whatever is before us" is the future time ahead of us, by "whatever is behind us" the past, and by "whatever is in between" the present. It was said: "whatever is before us" is the time before creation, "whatever is behind us" is what follows death until the continuity of the Hereafter, and "whatever is in between" is the duration of life. It was said: "whatever is before us" is the world until the first blast of the trumpet, "whatever is behind us" is what follows the second blast, and "whatever is in between" is the period between the two blasts, which is forty years. It was said: "whatever is before us" is the Hereafter and "whatever is behind us" is this world. It was said: "whatever is before us" is what preceded creation, "whatever is behind us" is what follows annihilation, and "whatever is in between" is what is between this world and the Hereafter. It was said: "whatever is before us" is what remains of the affairs of this world, "whatever is behind us" is what has passed of it, and "whatever is in between" is what they are currently in. It was said: the meaning is the beginning of our creation, the end of our lifespans, and the duration of our lives.

It was also said: "whatever is before us" is the heaven, "whatever is behind us" is the earth, and "whatever is in between" is what is between them. It was said: the opposite of that. It was said: "whatever is before us" is the place to which they move, "whatever is behind us" is the place from which they move, and "whatever is in between" is the place where they are.

The last three opinions share the view that the relative pronouns (الماءات) refer to spatial (مكانية) relations, just as the first seven share the view that they refer to temporal (زمانية) relations. There is also an opinion that the verse encompasses both time and space. These are eleven opinions, yet there is no evidence for any of them, in addition to the fact that they involve an analogy of the angel to the human being. The correct view is what we have previously stated.

Thus, His saying: لَهُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِينَا وَمَا خَلْفَنَا وَمَا بَيْنَ ذَلِكَ signifies the encompassment of His ownership (ملكه), the Exalted, over them—a real ownership (ملكا حقيقيا) in which no disposal by another nor the will of anyone else can occur except by His permission and volition. Since there is no disobedience (معصية) among the angels, they do not perform an act except by His command and after His permission, and they do not will except what Allah wills; thus, no angel descends except by the command of his Lord.

It has been established by this explanation that His saying: لَهُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِينَا وَمَا خَلْفَنَا وَمَا بَيْنَ ذَلِكَ stands in the position of providing the reason (التعليل) for His saying: وَمَا نَتَنَزَّلُ إِلَّا بِأَمْرِ رَبِّكَ. Furthermore, His saying: وَمَا كَانَ رَبُّكَ نَسِيًّا "and your Lord is not forgetful"—and nasiyy (نسيا) is an intensive form (فعول) of forgetfulness (النسيان)—is part of the completion of the reasoning. That is, He, the Exalted, does not forget anything of His kingdom such that the matter of governance (التدبير) would be disrupted by His neglect; thus, He would not fail to command descent when descent is necessary, or command it when it is not, and so on. This was the reason for the shift in the verse from affirming knowledge or remembrance to negating forgetfulness.

It was said the meaning of "and your Lord is not forgetful" is that He is not one who abandons His prophets; meaning, the lack of descent was only due to the lack of a command for it, and it was not due to His abandoning you or bidding you farewell.

However, while this agrees in a way with what was mentioned regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول), the reasoning provided by His saying: لَهُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِينَا etc., would remain incomplete, and His saying: رَبُّ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا would be disconnected from what preceded it, as will become clear.

His saying, the Exalted: رَبُّ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا فَاعْبُدْهُ وَاصْطَبِرْ لِعِبَادَتِهِ هَلْ تَعْلَمُ لَهُ سَمِيًّا "The Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them. So worship Him and be steadfast in His worship. Do you know any namesake for Him?" The beginning of the verse, namely His saying: رَبُّ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا, is a reasoning for His saying in the previous verse: لَهُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِينَا وَمَا خَلْفَنَا until the end of the verse. That is, how could He not own what is before us, what is behind us, and what is in between, and how could He be forgetful, when He, the Exalted, is the Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them? The Lord (الرب) of a thing is its Owner (المالك) who governs its affairs (المدبر لأمره); thus, His ownership and His lack of forgetfulness are the requirements of His Lordship (ربوبيته).

His saying: فَاعْبُدْهُ وَاصْطَبِرْ لِعِبَادَتِهِ "So worship Him and be steadfast in His worship" is a derivation (تفريع) from the beginning of the verse. The meaning is: since we do not descend except by the command of your Lord, and we have descended upon you with this speech which includes the call to His worship, then the speech is His speech and the call is His call; therefore, worship Him alone and be steadfast in His worship. For there is no one named "Lord" other than your Lord, such that you would not be steadfast in the worship of your Lord and move to the worship of that other who is named "Lord," sufficing with his worship instead of the worship of your Lord, or associating him with Him. It has been said that the sentence is a derivation from His saying: رَبُّ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ or from His saying: وَمَا كَانَ رَبُّكَ نَسِيًّا, meaning: "Your Lord has not forgotten you, so worship Him..." but both views are as you see [weak].

Several matters have become clear through this exposition:

First: His saying: هَلْ تَعْلَمُ لَهُ سَمِيًّا "Do you know any namesake for Him?" is part of the completion of the explanation intended by His saying: فَاعْبُدْهُ وَاصْطَبِرْ لِعِبَادَتِهِ, and it stands in the position of providing the reason for it.

Second: What is intended by "namesake" (السمي) is a partner in the name, and what is intended by the "name" is "the Lord" (الرب). This is because the requirement of the verse's explanation is the establishment of absolute Lordship for Him, the Exalted, over everything. Thus, He says: "Do you know anyone characterized by Lordship such that he is named 'Lord' because of it, so that you might turn away from Him to that other and worship him instead of Him?"

By this, the lack of soundness in most of what has been said regarding the meaning of "namesake" (السمي) in the verse becomes apparent. It was said: what is intended by "namesake" is "equal" (المماثل) metaphorically. It was said: "namesake" means "son" (الولد). It was said: it is in its literal sense, but the name in which there is no partnership is "Lord of the heavens and the earth." It was said: it is the Name of Majesty (اسم الجلالة) [Allah]. It was said: it is "the God" (الإله). It was said: it is "the All-Beneficent" (الرحمن). It was said: it is the God, the Creator, the Provider, the Giver of Life, the Bringer of Death, the One capable of reward and punishment.

Third: The subtle point (النكتة) in attributing "Lord" to the second-person pronoun and repeating it in the first verse—where He said: "by the command of your Lord" and said: "and your Lord was not"—rather than saying "our Lord," is to prepare for the monotheism (توحيد) of the Lord mentioned at the end of the discourse. In His saying: رَبِّكَ "your Lord," there is an indication that "our Lord, by whose command we descend, is your Lord; thus the call is His call, so worship Him." It is possible that this is the same subtle point in the opening of the Surah where He said: ذِكْرُ رَحْمَتِ رَبِّكَ "A mention of the mercy of your Lord..." because the verses, as we have pointed out, possess a single context for a single purpose.

Fourth: His saying: فَاعْبُدْهُ وَاصْطَبِرْ لِعِبَادَتِهِ is directed toward the monotheism of worship (توحيد العبادة) and is not merely a command for worship and a command for steadfastness and continuity in it, except by way of implication (الملازمة); so understand that.

It can be inferred from the derivation that it is a reinforcement of the explanation contained in the context preceding these two verses. By this, their connection to the verses preceding them becomes apparent without them being considered parenthetical in every respect.

It is as if the Angel of Revelation, when he descended upon him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله with the Surah and revealed the sixty-three verses of it—which contain a complete call to the upright religion (الدين الحنيف)—addressed him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله saying that he did not descend, nor does he ever descend with what he brings, from himself, but rather by a command from his Lord and with a message from Him. Thus, the speech is His speech and the call is His call, and He is the Lord of the Prophet and the Lord of everything; so let him worship Him alone, for there is no other Lord to turn to. Thus, the two verses are among what was revealed to the Angel of Revelation to deliver to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله as a strengthening for him and a reinforcement of the preceding verses.

This is similar to a king sending a messenger with a letter or a message to one of his officials. The messenger comes to him, and once he has read the letter or delivered the message, he says to the official: "I have not come to you of my own accord, but by the command of the king and his direction. The letter is his letter and the message is his speech and his decree. He is your sovereign and the sovereign of all who are in the kingdom; so listen to him, obey him, and remain steadfast in that, for there is no sovereign other than him to whom you might turn."

The speech of this messenger is a reinforcement of the king's speech. If we assume that the king is the one who commanded him to follow his message with this speech, then the speech would be the speech of the messenger and also a message on behalf of the king and his speech.

It is not hidden from you that this perspective is more in accord with the two verses and more clearly applicable to them than what is mentioned in the narrations regarding the occasion of revelation, given the discrepancies and weaknesses they contain.

Ayahs

وَيَقُولُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ أَءِذَا مَا مِتُّ لَسَوْفَ أُخْرَجُ حَيًّا (٦٦)
Man says, ‘What? Shall I be brought forth alive [from the grave], when I have been dead?’ (66)
أَوَلَا يَذْكُرُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ أَنَّا خَلَقْنَـٰهُ مِن قَبْلُ وَلَمْ يَكُ شَيْـًٔا (٦٧)
Does not man remember that We created him before when he was nothing? (67)
فَوَرَبِّكَ لَنَحْشُرَنَّهُمْ وَٱلشَّيَـٰطِينَ ثُمَّ لَنُحْضِرَنَّهُمْ حَوْلَ جَهَنَّمَ جِثِيًّا (٦٨)
By your Lord, We will surely gather them with the devils; then We will surely bring them up around hell [scrambling] on their knees. (68)
ثُمَّ لَنَنزِعَنَّ مِن كُلِّ شِيعَةٍ أَيُّهُمْ أَشَدُّ عَلَى ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ عِتِيًّا (٦٩)
Then from every group We shall draw whichever of them was more defiant towards the All-beneficent. (69)
ثُمَّ لَنَحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِٱلَّذِينَ هُمْ أَوْلَىٰ بِهَا صِلِيًّا (٧٠)
Then surely We will know best those who deserve most to enter it. (70)
وَإِن مِّنكُمْ إِلَّا وَارِدُهَا ۚ كَانَ عَلَىٰ رَبِّكَ حَتْمًا مَّقْضِيًّا (٧١)
There is none of you but will come to it: a [matter that is a] decided certainty with your Lord. (71)
ثُمَّ نُنَجِّى ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوا۟ وَّنَذَرُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ فِيهَا جِثِيًّا (٧٢)
Then We will deliver those who are Godwary, and leave the wrongdoers in it, fallen on their knees. (72)

(Commentary)

This is a return to what preceded His saying: وَمَا نَتَنَزَّلُ إِلَّا بِأَمْرِ رَبِّكَ — "We do not descend except by the command of your Lord" (19:64). The two verses and what followed in the previous discourse serve as a supplementary conclusion (تذنيب) to His saying: فَخَلَفَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ خَلْفٌ أَضَاعُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَاتَّبَعُوا الشَّهَوَاتِ فَسَوْفَ يَلْقَوْنَ غَيًّا — "But they were succeeded by a generations who neglected the prayer and followed their appetites. So they will soon encounter [the reward of] perversity" (19:59), by mentioning some of what they uttered out of their perversity (غي). He specifically mentioned one of their sayings regarding the Return (المعاد), another regarding Prophethood (النبوة), and another regarding the Origin (المبدأ).

In these verses—meaning His saying: وَيَقُولُ الْإِنْسَانُ — "The human says..." until His saying: وَنَذَرُ الظَّالِمِينَ فِيهَا جِثِيًّا — "...and We shall leave the wrongdoers in it, fallen on their knees" (19:66-72)—which are seven verses, He mentions their deeming the Resurrection (البعث) improbable, the response to it, and an indication of the consequences and evil aftermath (الوبال) of this saying of theirs.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَقُولُ الْإِنْسَانُ أَإِذَا مَا مِتُّ لَسَوْفَ أُخْرَجُ حَيًّا — "The human says, ‘What! Once I am dead, shall I really be brought forth alive?’" (19:66). This is a denial of the Resurrection in the form of deeming it improbable. It is the saying of the disbelievers (الكفار) among the idolaters and those who join them from the deniers of the Creator (الصانع); rather, it is something toward which the human nature (طبع الإنسان) inclines before referring to proof. It has been said: For this reason, the saying was attributed to "the human" (الإنسان) when the requirement of the context was to say: "The disbeliever says," or "Those who disbelieve say," etc. However, this is problematic as it does not align with His subsequent saying: فَوَرَبِّكَ لَنَحْشُرَنَّهُمْ وَالشَّيَاطِينَ — "By your Lord, We will surely gather them and the devils..." until His saying: صِلِيًّا — "...burning" (19:68-70).

It is not far-fetched that the intended meaning of "the human" who says this is the disbeliever who denies the Resurrection. He is referred to as "the human" because such a thing is not expected from him, given that Allah the Exalted has equipped him with intellectual perception (الإدراك العقلي), and he remembers that Allah created him before when he was nothing. Thus, it is not far-fetched that He should return him a second time; so his deeming it improbable is itself improbable for him. Therefore, the word "human" is repeated when the response begins, saying: أَوَلَا يَذْكُرُ الْإِنْسَانُ أَنَّا خَلَقْنَاهُ مِنْ قَبْلُ وَلَمْ يَكُ شَيْئًا — "Does not the human remember that We created him before when he was nothing?" (19:67). That is, he is a human who ought not to deem improbable the occurrence of something the like of which he has witnessed occurring, and he is not forgetful of it.

Perhaps the use of the present tense (المضارع) in His saying: وَيَقُولُ الْإِنْسَانُ — "The human says" is to indicate the persistence of this improbability among the deniers of the Return and those who doubt it.

His saying, the Exalted: أَوَلَا يَذْكُرُ الْإِنْسَانُ أَنَّا خَلَقْنَاهُ مِنْ قَبْلُ وَلَمْ يَكُ شَيْئًا — "Does not the human remember that We created him before when he was nothing?" (19:67). The interrogative is for expressing wonder and deeming their stance improbable. The meaning of the verse is clear; it proceeds by removing the improbability by mentioning the occurrence of a similar instance (المثل) to establish possibility (الإمكان). Thus, the verse is similar to His saying, the Exalted, in another place: وَضَرَبَ لَنَا مَثَلًا وَنَسِيَ خَلْقَهُ قَالَ مَنْ يُحْيِي الْعِظَامَ وَهِيَ رَمِيمٌ قُلْ يُحْيِيهَا الَّذِي أَنْشَأَهَا أَوَّلَ مَرَّةٍ — "He draws an example for Us, and forgets his own creation. He says, ‘Who shall revive the bones when they have decayed?’ Say, ‘He shall revive them who produced them the first time...’" until He said: أَوَلَيْسَ الَّذِي خَلَقَ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ بِقَادِرٍ عَلَى أَنْ يَخْلُقَ مِثْلَهُمْ — "Is not He who created the heavens and the earth able to create the like of them?" (36:78-81).

If it is asked: Arguing by the occurrence of a like (المثل) only results in the possibility of a like, whereas what is required in proving the Return is the return of the human in his specific person and essence (بعينه), not just a like, for the like of a thing is other than it. It is replied: These verses are in the process of proving the return of the bodies (الأجساد), and that which is created a second time is a like of that which was created the first time. However, the personhood (شخصية) of the human individual is by his soul (النفس), not by his body. Thus, when the body is created a second time and the soul attaches to it, it is the very same worldly human person, even if the body—which is a part of the human—is a like (مثل) in comparison to the worldly body, not the identical matter (عينا). This is just as the personhood and unity of the human are preserved in this world throughout his life despite the change and replacement of the body through the change and replacement of its parts state after state; the body in the second state is other than it was in the first state, yet the human remains in both states in his personal unity (وحدته الشخصية) due to the persistence of his soul in its specific identity.

To this points His saying, the Exalted: وَقَالُوا أَإِذَا ضَلَلْنَا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَإِنَّا لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ — "They say, ‘When we have been lost in the earth, shall we be in a new creation?’" until He said: قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ مَلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ — "Say, ‘The angel of death, who has been given charge of you, will take you...’" (32:10-11). That is, you are taken from your bodies and preserved; you are not lost nor do you go missing.

His saying, the Exalted: فَوَرَبِّكَ لَنَحْشُرَنَّهُمْ وَالشَّيَاطِينَ ثُمَّ لَنُحْضِرَنَّهُمْ حَوْلَ جَهَنَّمَ جِثِيًّا — "By your Lord, We will surely gather them and the devils; then We will surely bring them around hell, fallen on their knees" (19:68). The word جِثِيًّا (jithiyya) is originally on the pattern of fu‘ūl, the plural of jāthī, which refers to one kneeling on his knees. It is attributed to Ibn Abbas that it is the plural of juthwah, which is a gathered heap of soil and stones. The meaning would then be that they are brought in crowds and groups, piled upon one another. This meaning is more suitable to the context.

The plural pronoun in لَنَحْشُرَنَّهُمْ ("We will surely gather them") and لَنُحْضِرَنَّهُمْ ("We will surely bring them") refers to the disbelievers. The verse, along with the following two verses, addresses their state on the Day of Resurrection, which is apparent. It has been said that the two pronouns refer to people in general, including both the believer and the disbeliever, just as the pronoun of address in His subsequent saying: وَإِنْ مِنْكُمْ إِلَّا وَارِدُهَا — "There is none of you but he shall come to it" (19:71) is likewise. However, the tone of the three verses—which is a tone of wrath and punishment—rejects this.

The meaning of His saying: لَنَحْشُرَنَّهُمْ وَالشَّيَاطِينَ — "We will surely gather them and the devils" is their gathering outside the graves with their allies (أولياء) from the devils, because due to their lack of faith, they are perverse (غاوون), as He said: فَسَوْفَ يَلْقَوْنَ غَيًّا — "So they will soon encounter [the reward of] perversity" (19:59). The devils are their allies; the Exalted said: إِنَّ عِبَادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَانٌ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغَاوِينَ — "Indeed, as for My servants, you shall have no authority over them, except such of the perverse as follow you" (15:42), and He said: إِنَّا جَعَلْنَا الشَّيَاطِينَ أَوْلِيَاءَ لِلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ — "Indeed We have made the devils the allies of those who do not have faith" (7:27). Or it means their gathering with their companions (قرناء) from the devils, as He said: وَمَنْ يَعْشُ عَنْ ذِكْرِ الرَّحْمَنِ نُقَيِّضْ لَهُ شَيْطَانًا فَهُوَ لَهُ قَرِينٌ... حَتَّى إِذَا جَاءَنَا قَالَ يَا لَيْتَ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكَ بُعْدَ الْمَشْرِقَيْنِ فَبِئْسَ الْقَرِينُ وَلَنْ يَنْفَعَكُمُ الْيَوْمَ إِذْ ظَلَمْتُمْ أَنَّكُمْ فِي الْعَذَابِ مُشْتَرِكُونَ — "Whoever turns away from the remembrance of the All-beneficent, We assign him a devil who becomes his companion... When he comes to Us, he will say, ‘I wish there were between me and you the distance between the east and the west! What an evil companion!’ [They will be told,] ‘It will not benefit you today, since you were unjust, that you are partners in the punishment’" (43:36-39).

The meaning is: I swear by your Lord, We shall surely gather them—on the Day of Resurrection—and their allies or companions from the devils, then We shall surely bring them around Hell to taste the punishment while they are kneeling on their knees out of humiliation, or while they are in groups and crowds.

In His saying: فَوَرَبِّكَ — "By your Lord," there is a transition (التفات) from speaking in the first person plural to the third person. Perhaps the subtle point (النكتة) in this is what was mentioned previously regarding His saying: بِأَمْرِ رَبِّكَ — "by the command of your Lord," and its like is His subsequent saying: كَانَ عَلَى رَبِّكَ حَتْمًا — "it is a decided certainty [incumbent] on your Lord."

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ لَنَنْزِعَنَّ مِنْ كُلِّ شِيعَةٍ أَيُّهُمْ أَشَدُّ عَلَى الرَّحْمَنِ عِتِيًّا — "Then We will surely extract from every group those of them who were most rebellious against the All-beneficent" (19:69). النزع (al-naz‘) is extraction. الشيعة (al-shī‘ah) is a group cooperating on a matter or following a creed. العتي (al-‘utiyy) is a verbal noun on the pattern of fu‘ūl meaning defiance in disobedience. It is apparent that His saying: أَيُّهُمْ أَشَدُّ عَلَى الرَّحْمَنِ عِتِيًّا — "those of them who were most rebellious against the All-beneficent" is an interrogative sentence placed in the position of the object of "We will surely extract" (لَنَنْزِعَنَّ) to indicate the concern for identification and distinction. It is thus similar to His saying: أُولَئِكَ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ يَبْتَغُونَ إِلَى رَبِّهِمُ الْوَسِيلَةَ أَيُّهُمْ أَقْرَبُ — "They [themselves] are the ones who supplicate, seeking a recourse to their Lord, [to see] which of them is nearest" (17:57).

The meaning is: Then We shall surely extract from every formed group those most defiant against the All-Beneficent (الرحمن), and they are the leaders and the imams of misguidance. It has also been said the meaning is: We shall extract the most defiant, then the next most defiant, until they are all encompassed.

In His saying: عَلَى الرَّحْمَنِ — "against the All-beneficent," there is a transition (التفات), and the subtle point is an allusion that their defiance is grave because it is defiance against Him Whose mercy encompasses everything, and they have received nothing from Him but mercy; defiance against One of such a status is grave indeed.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ لَنَحْنُ أَعْلَمُ بِالَّذِينَ هُمْ أَوْلَى بِهَا صِلِيًّا — "Then We will surely know best those who are most deserving to burn in it" (19:70). الصلي (al-ṣiliy) is originally a verbal noun on the pattern of fu‘ūl; it is said "he burned in the fire" (صلي النار) when he suffers its heat. The meaning is: Then I swear We are most knowing of who is most deserving of the fire to suffer its heat; that is, the matter of the levels (دركات) of their punishment and the degrees of their desert will not be confused for Us.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنْ مِنْكُمْ إِلَّا وَارِدُهَا كَانَ عَلَى رَبِّكَ حَتْمًا مَقْضِيًّا — "There is none of you but he shall come to it: a decided certainty [incumbent] on your Lord" (19:71). The address is to people in general, their believers and their disbelievers, as evidenced by His saying in the following verse: ثُمَّ نُنَجِّي الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا — "Then We will deliver those who were God-conscious" (19:72). The pronoun in وَارِدُهَا ("come to it") refers to the Fire. It has been said that the address is to the disbelievers mentioned in the three preceding verses, and in the speech is a transition (التفات) from the third person to the second person; however, the context of the following verse rejects this.

الورود (al-wurūd) is the opposite of al-ṣudūr (leaving), and it is the seeking of water as appears from the books of linguistics. Al-Raghib said in al-Mufradat: "Al-Wurūd is originally the seeking of water, then it is used for other things. It is said: 'I came to the water' (وردت الماء) ... and the camels were brought to the water (أوردت الإبل الماء). The Exalted said: وَلَمَّا وَرَدَ مَاءَ مَدْيَنَ — 'And when he came to the waters of Midian' (28:23). Al-Wird is the water prepared for coming to... and al-wird is the day of fever when it comes. It is used for the Fire by way of terror; the Exalted said: فَأَوْرَدَهُمُ النَّارَ — 'and he led them into the Fire' (11:98), وَبِئْسَ الْوِرْدُ الْمَوْرُودُ — 'and evil is the place to which they are led' (11:98), إِلَى جَهَنَّمَ وِرْدًا — 'to hell, as a thirsty herd' (19:86), أَنْتُمْ لَهَا وَارِدُونَ — 'you shall enter it' (21:98), مَا وَرَدُوهَا — 'they would not have entered it' (21:99). Al-Wārid is the one who precedes the people to draw water for them; the Exalted said: فَأَرْسَلُوا وَارِدَهُمْ — 'so they sent their water-drawer' (12:19)." [End of the relevant portion].

Based on this, some exegetes have argued that people will only be present at the Fire and overlook it without entering it. They cited as evidence His saying, the Exalted: وَلَمَّا وَرَدَ مَاءَ مَدْيَنَ وَجَدَ عَلَيْهِ أُمَّةً مِنَ النَّاسِ يَسْقُونَ — "And when he came to the waters of Midian, he found there a group of people watering [their flocks]" (28:23), and His saying: فَأَرْسَلُوا وَارِدَهُمْ فَأَدْلَى دَلْوَهُ — "so they sent their water-drawer, and he let down his bucket" (12:19), and His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ سَبَقَتْ لَهُمْ مِنَّا الْحُسْنَى أُولَئِكَ عَنْهَا مُبْعَدُونَ لَا يَسْمَعُونَ حَسِيسَهَا — "Indeed those to whom the [promise of] the best reward has gone ahead from Us, they shall be kept far from it. They will not hear its sound" (21:101-102).

The response to this is that its use in verses like وَلَمَّا وَرَدَ مَاءَ مَدْيَنَ and فَأَرْسَلُوا وَارِدَهُمْ to mean presence (الحضور) by the association of overlooking (الإشراف) does not preclude its use for entry (الدخول) in a literal sense (على نحو الحقيقة) as claimed in other verses. As for His saying: أُولَئِكَ عَنْهَا مُبْعَدُونَ لَا يَسْمَعُونَ حَسِيسَهَا, it is possible that the distancing occurs after the entry, as may be inferred from His saying: ثُمَّ نُنَجِّي الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا وَنَذَرُ الظَّالِمِينَ فِيهَا — "Then We will deliver those who were God-conscious, and We shall leave the wrongdoers in it" (19:72), and that Allah veils them from hearing its sound as an honor to them, just as He veiled Ibrahim from the heat of the fire when He said to the fire: كُونِي بَرْدًا وَسَلَامًا عَلَى إِبْرَاهِيمَ — "Be coolness and peace for Ibrahim" (21:69).

Others—and they are perhaps the majority of exegetes—argued that the verse indicates their entry into the Fire, relying on verses such as His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّكُمْ وَمَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ حَصَبُ جَهَنَّمَ أَنْتُمْ لَهَا وَارِدُونَ لَوْ كَانَ هَؤُلَاءِ آلِهَةً مَا وَرَدُوهَا — "Indeed you and what you worship besides Allah are the fuel of hell, and you will enter it. Had these been gods, they would not have entered it" (21:98-99), and His saying regarding Pharaoh: يَقْدُمُ قَوْمَهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ فَأَوْرَدَهُمُ النَّارَ — "He will lead his people on the Day of Resurrection and lead them into the Fire" (11:98). This is also indicated by His saying in the following verse: ثُمَّ نُنَجِّي الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا وَنَذَرُ الظَّالِمِينَ فِيهَا جِثِيًّا — "Then We will deliver those who were God-conscious, and We shall leave the wrongdoers in it, fallen on their knees" (19:72). That is, We leave them kneeling; and "leaving" (نذر / نترك) is only said of one who was inside and established in the place before being left, then remained as he was. This is also supported by several narrations (روايات) regarding the interpretation of the verse.

These scholars are divided between those who say all people will enter it and those who say only those other than the God-conscious will enter, claiming that His saying مِنْكُمْ ("of you") means "of them" (منهم), similar to His saying: وَسَقَاهُمْ رَبُّهُمْ شَرَابًا طَهُورًا إِنَّ هَذَا كَانَ لَكُمْ جَزَاءً — "and their Lord will give them to drink a pure beverage. [They will be told,] ‘This is your reward...’" (76:21-22). However, this does not suit the context of His saying: ثُمَّ نُنَجِّي الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا — "Then We will deliver those who were God-conscious."

The response to this is that the use of al-wurūd in verses like لَوْ كَانَ هَؤُلَاءِ آلِهَةً مَا وَرَدُوهَا to mean entry is contested; rather, it is more appropriate for it to mean presence and overlooking, as that is more eloquent, as is apparent. Likewise in His saying: فَأَوْرَدَهُمُ النَّارَ, for the role of Pharaoh—who is one of the imams of misguidance—is to lead his people to the Fire, but as for entering them into it, that is not up to him.

As for His saying: ثُمَّ نُنَجِّي الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا وَنَذَرُ الظَّالِمِينَ فِيهَا, the verse indicates their being inside it by the evidence of His saying نَذَرُ ("We leave"). However, its indication of their being inside is distinct from the word وَارِدُهَا ("come to it") being used to mean entry. Similarly, the deliverance (تنجية) of the God-conscious does not necessitate their having been inside it, for deliverance is as applicable to saving one who has entered a place of peril as it is to distancing one who has overlooked destruction and was present at the place of peril.

As for the narrations, they were only mentioned to describe the event, not to specify the linguistic usage of the word وَارِدُهَا in the verse; thus, arguing from them that al-wurūd means entry is invalid.

If you say: Why is it not possible that the intended meaning is the potentiality (شأنية) of entry? The meaning would then be: There is none of you but that it is his lot to enter the Fire, and only those who are saved are saved by the deliverance of Allah, similar to His saying: وَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُ مَا زَكَى مِنْكُمْ مِنْ أَحَدٍ أَبَدًا — "Were it not for Allah’s grace and His mercy upon you, not one of you would ever be pure" (24:21).

I say: This would mean that al-wurūd is the requirement of human nature (طبع الإنسان) in the sense that whatever good and felicity he attains is from Allah, and nothing remains for him from himself but evil and wretchedness. However, this is contradicted by what is at the end of the verse: كَانَ عَلَى رَبِّكَ حَتْمًا مَقْضِيًّا — "it is a decided certainty [incumbent] on your Lord," for it is explicit that this al-wurūd is by an act of bringing (إيراد) from Allah and by His decreed judgment, not by the requirement of the nature of things.

The truth is that al-wurūd does not indicate more than presence and overlooking with intent—as is gathered from the books of linguistics. Thus, His saying: وَإِنْ مِنْكُمْ إِلَّا وَارِدُهَا only indicates the intent, presence, and overlooking. This does not contradict the indication of His saying in the following verse: ثُمَّ نُنَجِّي الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا وَنَذَرُ الظَّالِمِينَ فِيهَا جِثِيًّا regarding their all entering, or the entry of the wrongdoers specifically, after they have come to it (وردوها).

In His saying: كَانَ عَلَى رَبِّكَ حَتْمًا مَقْضِيًّا, the pronoun in كَانَ ("it is") refers to the al-wurūd or to the preceding sentence considering it is a ruling. الحتم (al-ḥatm), الجزم (al-jazm), and القطع (al-qaṭ‘) all carry the same meaning of certainty; that is, this al-wurūd or ruling was obligatory upon Him, the Exalted, decreed in His right. He only decreed that Himself upon Himself, for there is no judge to rule over Him.

His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ نُنَجِّي الَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا وَنَذَرُ الظَّالِمِينَ فِيهَا جِثِيًّا — "Then We will deliver those who were God-conscious, and We shall leave the wrongdoers in it, fallen on their knees" (19:72). It has already been pointed out that His saying وَنَذَرُ الظَّالِمِينَ فِيهَا indicates that the wrongdoers are inside it, then they are left as they were. As for the deliverance of those who were God-conscious, its wording does not indicate their being inside, as deliverance can be realized without it, unless that is inferred from the two terms appearing together in a single context.

In the expression "the wrongdoers" (الظالمين), there is an indication that the attribute is the cause (علية) for the judgment.

The meaning of the two verses is: There is none of you—God-conscious or wrongdoer—but he shall come to the Fire; this bringing was an obligatory decree upon your Lord. Then We shall deliver those who were God-conscious from it and leave the wrongdoers in it due to their wrongdoing, kneeling on their knees.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Kafi, with its chain of transmission to Malik al-Juhani, who said: "I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: أَوَلَا يَذْكُرُ الْإِنْسَانُ أَنَّا خَلَقْنَاهُ مِنْ قَبْلُ وَلَمْ يَكُ شَيْئًا — 'Does not the human remember that We created him before when he was nothing?' (19:67). He said: 'Neither determined (مقدرا) nor written (مكتوبا).'"

In al-Mahasin, with its chain of transmission to Humran, who said: "I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about His saying: أَوَلَا يَذْكُرُ الْإِنْسَانُ — the verse. He said: 'He was not in a book nor in knowledge.'"

I say: The meaning of the two traditions is that he was not in a book or knowledge among the books of erasure and affirmation (المحو والإثبات), then Allah affirmed him when He willed his existence. As for the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ), nothing escapes it according to the text of the Quran.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: ثُمَّ لَنُحْضِرَنَّهُمْ حَوْلَ جَهَنَّمَ جِثِيًّا, he said: "On their knees."

In the same source, with its chain of transmission to al-Husayn ibn Abi al-Ala, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): Regarding His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: وَإِنْ مِنْكُمْ إِلَّا وَارِدُهَا, he said: "Do you not hear a man say: 'We came to (وردنا) the tribe of so-and-so'? That is the al-wurūd, and he did not enter it."

In al-Majma‘, from al-Suddi, who said: "I asked Murrah al-Hamdani about this verse, and he narrated to me that Abdullah ibn Mas‘ud narrated to them from the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny), who said: يرد الناس النار ثم يصدرون بأعمالهم ـ فأولهم كلمع البرق ثم كمر الريح ثم كحضر الفرس ـ ثم كالراكب ثم كشد الرجل ثم كمشيه — 'People will come to the Fire, then they will leave according to their deeds. The first of them like a flash of lightning, then like the passing of the wind, then like the gallop of a horse, then like a rider, then like a man running, then like his walking.'"

In the same source: Abu Salih Ghalib ibn Sulayman narrated from Kathir ibn Ziyad from Abu Sumayyah, who said: "We differed regarding al-wurūd. A group said: 'No believer shall enter it.' Others said: 'They shall all enter it, then Allah will deliver those who were God-conscious.' So I met Jabir ibn Abdillah and asked him. He pointed with his two fingers to his ears and said: 'May they be deaf if I did not hear the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny) saying: الورود الدخول لا يبقى بر ولا فاجر حتى يدخلها ـ فتكون على المؤمنين بردا وسلاما ـ كما كانت على إبراهيم ـ حتى أن للنار ـ أو قال : لجهنم ـ ضجيجا من بردها ـ ثم ينجي الله الذين اتقوا ـ ويذر الظالمين فيها جثيا — "The al-wurūd is the entry. No righteous or wicked person will remain but he shall enter it. It will be coolness and peace for the believers, just as it was for Ibrahim, until the Fire—or he said: Hell—will have a clamor from its coldness. Then Allah will deliver those who were God-conscious and leave the wrongdoers in it, fallen on their knees."'"

I say: The narration is of the nature of exegesis, but its chain is weak due to unknown narrators (الجهالة).

In the same source: It is narrated as a marfū‘ tradition from Ya‘la ibn Munabbih from the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his progeny), who said: تقول النار للمؤمن يوم القيامة : جز يا مؤمن فقد أطفأ نورك لهبي — "The Fire will say to the believer on the Day of Resurrection: 'Pass, O believer, for your light has extinguished my flame.'"

In the same source: It is narrated from the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) that he was asked about the meaning, and he said: إن الله يجعل النار كالسمن الجامد ـ ويجمع عليها الخلق ثم ينادي المنادي ـ أن خذي أصحابك وذري أصحابي ـ فو الذي نفسي بيده ـ لهي أعرف بأصحابها من الوالدة بولدها — "Indeed, Allah will make the Fire like solidified clarified butter and gather the creation upon it. Then a caller will cry out: 'Take your companions and leave My companions.' By Him in Whose hand is my soul, it knows its companions better than a mother knows her child."

I say: The last four narrations were narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from several authors of books and collections, except that in the second narration—in the version of al-Durr al-Manthur available to us—his saying "The al-wurūd is the entry" is not mentioned.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Abu Nu‘aym narrated in al-Hilyah from Urwah ibn al-Zubayr, who said: "When Ibn Rawahah intended to go out to the land of Mu'tah in Sham, the Muslims came to bid him farewell, and he wept. He said: 'By Allah, I have no love for the world nor longing for you, but I heard the Messenger of Allah recite this verse: وَإِنْ مِنْكُمْ إِلَّا وَارِدُهَا كَانَ عَلَى رَبِّكَ حَتْمًا مَقْضِيًّا — so I know that I am coming to the Fire, but I do not know how the leaving (الصدور) will be after the coming (الورود).'"

Know that the apparent meaning of some of the preceding narrations is that the people's coming to the Fire is their passing over it; thus, it aligns with the narrations of the Bridge (الصراط). In them, it is a bridge extended over the Fire, which both the righteous and the wicked are commanded to cross; the righteous pass over it, and the wicked fall into it. It is narrated from al-Saduq in al-I‘tiqad that he interpreted the verse accordingly.

It is said in Majma‘ al-Bayan: "It has been said that the benefit in that—meaning the coming to the Fire—is what was narrated in some reports: That Allah the Exalted does not enter anyone into Paradise until He shows him the Fire and the punishment within it, so that he may know the full extent of Allah's grace upon him and the perfection of His favor and kindness toward him, thereby increasing his joy and delight in Paradise and its bliss. And He does not enter anyone into the Fire until He shows him Paradise and the types of bliss and reward within it, so that it may be an additional punishment for him and a source of regret for what he missed of Paradise and its bliss." [End].

(A Discourse on the Meaning of the Necessity, Possibility, and Impossibility of an Act for Allah, the Glorified)

It has already been mentioned in the first volume of the book, under His saying, the Exalted: وَمَا يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفَاسِقِينَ — "and He does not mislead by it any except the transgressors" (2:26), in a Quranic discussion, that He, the Exalted, possesses absolute ownership (الملك المطلق) over things. This means He owns everything with an absolute ownership that is not restricted by state, time, or any assumed condition. Everything is owned by Him, the Exalted, without being owned by Him from one aspect and not owned from another, neither in its essence (ذاته) nor in anything related to it.

Thus, He, the Exalted, may act as He wills in whatever He wills, without that entailing ugliness (قبح), blame, or vilification from the intellect (عقل) or otherwise. This is because ugliness or blame only attaches to an agent if he performs an act he does not own by the judgment of intellect, law, or established custom. But if he performs what he has the right to do and what he owns, no ugliness, blame, or reproach can ever affect him. In human society, there is no absolute ownership or absolute freedom, as it contradicts the meaning of society and the sharing of benefits; thus, every ownership within it is restricted and limited. A person is blamed if he exceeds it, and his act is deemed ugly; he is praised if he restricts himself to it, and his work is deemed good.

This is in contrast to His ownership, the Exalted, for it is absolute, unrestricted, and unlimited, as indicated by the absolute nature of the verses of ownership (آيات الملك). This is supported—rather, proven—by the verses indicating the exclusivity of judgment and the restriction of legislation to Him, and the universality of His decree (قضاء) for everything. For were it not for the vastness of His ownership and the universality of His authority over everything, His judgment in everything and His decree in every event would not be sound. Arguing for the limitation of His ownership, the Exalted, based on rational ugliness—by saying that we see a master of a slave is blamed if he punishes his slave in a way the intellect does not permit, and the rational people deem his act ugly—is like arguing for a thing by the judgment of what is disparate from it.

Furthermore, this ownership that we affirm for Him, the Exalted—which is a legislative ownership (ملك تشريعي)—is that He, the Exalted, is such that the existence of everything terminates in Him. Or if you wish, say: the existence of everything is such that it is sustained by Him, the Exalted. This is the ontological ownership (الملك التكويني) from which no thing is excluded. That being the case, how can ontological ownership be realized in something without a legislative ownership and a designated right emanating from it? Unless it be from the privative concepts (العناوين العدمية) to which creation does not attach, such as the concepts of sins in the acts of servants, which return to the violation of the command and the failure to observe benefit (المصلحة) and wisdom (الحكمة). None of that is realized in what is considered an act of His, the Exalted; so reflect well upon this.

From this discussion, it follows that it is meaningless for anyone other than Him, the Exalted, to necessitate (يوجب) anything upon Him, or to prohibit or permit—in short, to charge Him with a legislative obligation (تكليف تشريعي)—just as it is impossible for anything to exert an ontological influence (تأثير تكويني) upon Him. This is because it would necessitate Him, the Exalted, being actually owned by that thing and falling under its authority regarding His act to which the obligation attached, which leads to the ownership of His essence, and that is impossible.

What is it that could dictate to Him, the Exalted? And who is it that could compel Him with an obligation? If it is assumed to be the intellect (العقل) that judges by its essence and decrees for itself, the discussion returns to the intellect's ownership of this judgment and decree. Yet the intellect relies in its judgments on matters external to its essence, such as benefits and harms; thus, it is not a judge by its essence but for something else.

If it is assumed to be the benefit (المصلحة) established with the intellect—for instance, that such a benefit requires Him, the Exalted, to be just in His judgment and not to wrong His servants, and then the intellect, after considering it, judges Him with the necessity of justice and the impermissibility of wrongdoing, or the goodness of justice and the ugliness of wrongdoing—then this benefit is either a conventional matter (أمر اعتباري) that is not real nor an actual existent, but rather something the intellect has constructed without it terminating in an external reality, in which case the matter returns to the intellect being a judge by its essence without relying in its judgment on a matter external to its essence, the falsity of which has already been mentioned.

Or it is a real matter (أمر حقيقي) existing externally, and it must necessarily be a contingent being (ممكنة) caused by the Necessary Being (الواجب), its existence terminating in Him, the Exalted, and sustained by Him. In that case, it would be one of His acts, and the matter would return to some of His acts, by their realization, preventing the realization of others, and that He thereby guided the intellect to judge the necessity of the act or its impermissibility. In other words, the matter terminates in Him, the Exalted, considering the system of creation, choosing one of His acts over another—which is that which contains the benefit over that which is devoid of it. This is according to the ontological order (التكوين). Then He guided the intellect to derive from the benefit that the act He chose—which is justice, for example—is obligatory upon Him. Or if you wish, say: He judged through the tongue of the intellect the necessity of the act upon Himself. In short, the necessitation (الإيجاب) did not terminate in another, but returned to Him; so He is the One Who necessitates upon Himself, not another.

Thus, several matters have become clear through this discussion:

First: That He possesses absolute ownership (ملك مطلق) that is not restricted by one disposal over another. He may do what He wills and judge as He intends. The Exalted said: فَعَّالٌ لِمَا يُرِيدُ — "All-doer of what He wills" (85:16), and He said: وَاللَّهُ يَحْكُمُ لَا مُعَقِّبَ لِحُكْمِهِ — "Allah judges, and there is no reviser of His judgment" (13:41). However, since He addressed us in the stage of guidance according to the capacity of our intellects and placed Himself in the position of legislation (التشريع), He necessitated certain things upon Himself and prohibited Himself from others. Thus, He deemed certain things good for Himself, such as justice and kindness, just as He deemed them good for us; and He deemed certain things ugly, such as wrongdoing and aggression, just as He deemed them ugly for us.

The meaning of Him, the Exalted, being a Legislator, Commander, and Prohibitor is that He decreed our existence within a precise system that links it to ends which are our felicity, and in which lies the good of our worldly life and our hereafter—these are called "benefits" (المصالح). He organized the causes of our existence and the apparatuses of our souls in an organization that only suits a specific course in life consisting of acts and deeds that are compatible with the benefits of our existence and nothing else. Thus, the causes of existence, the equipped apparatuses, and the circumstances and states surrounding us push us toward the benefits of our existence. The benefits of existence call us to specific acts that suit them and a course in life that leads us to the perfection of existence and the felicity of life. Or if you wish, say: they invite us to laws and traditions (سنن) in which, by acting upon them and following them, lies the good of this world and the hereafter.

These laws, which the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — calls out and which the heavenly revelation teaches, are the Sharia (الشريعة). Since it terminates in Him, the Exalted, the command within it is His command, the prohibition within it is His prohibition, and every judgment within it is His judgment. Within it are matters whose characterization of an act is seen as good in every case, such as justice; so He approves of it for His own act just as He approves of it for our acts. And there are matters He deems ugly, vile, and blames acts characterized by them, such as wrongdoing; so He does not approve of it for His act just as He does not approve of it for our acts, and so on.

So there is no harm in something being obligatory upon Him, the Exalted, with a legislative necessity (وجوب تشريعي) if He is the Legislator upon Himself. These are conventional judgments (أحكام اعتبارية) established in the domain of rational convention (الاعتبار العقلي), and their reality is that it is from His ontological tradition (سنته التكوينية) to will and perform matters which, when presented to the intellect, it labels as justice; and that no acts proceed from His presence which, when presented to the intellect, it labels as wrongdoing. So understand that.

Second: That this necessity is legislative (تشريعي), and there is another ontological necessity (وجوب تكويني) upon which this necessity relies. This is the necessity of the sequence of effects (المعلولات) upon their causes (عللها) in the general system without fail, from which the meaning of justice is derived.

The matter has been confused for many researchers who imagined this necessity to be ontological. They formulated it by saying that the Divine Power (القدرة الواجبية) is absolute and equally related to performing an ugly act and leaving it, for example; however, He never performs the ugly act due to His wisdom (حكمته). Thus, leaving the ugly act is necessary in relation to His wisdom, even if it is possible in relation to His power. This necessity is a real necessity (ضرورة حقيقية), like the necessity of our saying: "One is necessarily half of two," which is different from the conventional necessity (الوجوب الاعتباري) considered in masterly commands.

The fallacy in this is evident. For if leaving the ugly act were possible in relation to the Power, and the Power is identical to the Essence (عين الذات), then it would be possible in relation to the Essence. If the attribute of Wisdom were then identical to the Essence, leaving the ugly act would be both necessary and possible at the same time in relation to the Essence, which is nothing but a contradiction. If it were other than the Essence, then if it were an objective matter (أمر عيني) and had made the leaving necessary for the Essence after it was possible for it, it would necessitate the influence of something other than the Divine Essence upon it, which is another contradiction—and they only turned away from the view of legislative necessity to the view of ontological necessity to escape the necessity of another's authority over Him, the Exalted, through command and prohibition. If it were a derivative matter (أمر انتزاعي), then its being derived from the Essence leads to the first mentioned contradiction, and its being derived from something else leads to the second contradiction, for the real judgment in derivative matters belongs to the source of their derivation (منشأ انتزاعها).

The fallacy only arose from taking the act as necessary in relation to the Essence after the addition of Wisdom to it. For if the Essence is taken as the complete cause (علة تامة) for the act, the relation is necessity before the addition and after it, not possibility. And if it is taken as a part of the complete cause, which only becomes complete by the addition of a matter or matters to it, then the act in relation to it would be possible, not necessary, even if it were necessary, not possible, in relation to its complete cause composed of the Essence and other things.

Third: That our saying "Such is obligatory upon Him" and "Such is not permissible for Him" is a rational judgment (حكم عقلي). The intellect in this is a judge and decreer (حاكم قاض), not merely a perceiver and receiver (مدرك آخذ). This means that the judgment in the proposition is an act subsisting in the intellect and established by it, not a matter subsisting in itself which the intellect merely reports in some way, as has occurred in the language of some who said that its function is perception (الإدراك) rather than judgment (الحكم).

This is because the intellect we are discussing is the practical intellect (العقل العملي), whose domain of operation is action in terms of what "ought" or "ought not" to be, and what is "permissible" or "not permissible." Meanings of this nature are conventional matters (أمور اعتبارية) that have no realization in the external world outside the domain of intellection and perception. Thus, this perceptual establishment is itself an act of the intellect subsisting in it, which is the meaning of judgment and decree. As for the theoretical intellect (العقل النظري), whose domain of operation is real meanings (المعاني الحقيقية)—not conventional ones—whether in conception or assent, its objects of perception have an establishment in themselves independent of the intellect. Thus, nothing remains for the intellect upon perceiving them except to take them and report them, which is perception alone, not judgment and decree.

Ayahs

وَإِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتُنَا بَيِّنَـٰتٍ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لِلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ أَىُّ ٱلْفَرِيقَيْنِ خَيْرٌ مَّقَامًا وَأَحْسَنُ نَدِيًّا (٧٣)
When Our manifest signs are recited to them, the faithless say to the faithful, ‘Which of the two groups is superior in station and better with respect to company?’ (73)
وَكَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا قَبْلَهُم مِّن قَرْنٍ هُمْ أَحْسَنُ أَثَـٰثًا وَرِءْيًا (٧٤)
How many a generation We have destroyed before them, who were superior in furnishings and appearance! (74)
قُلْ مَن كَانَ فِى ٱلضَّلَـٰلَةِ فَلْيَمْدُدْ لَهُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ مَدًّا ۚ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا رَأَوْا۟ مَا يُوعَدُونَ إِمَّا ٱلْعَذَابَ وَإِمَّا ٱلسَّاعَةَ فَسَيَعْلَمُونَ مَنْ هُوَ شَرٌّ مَّكَانًا وَأَضْعَفُ جُندًا (٧٥)
Say, ‘Whoever abides in error, the All-beneficent shall prolong his respite until they sight what they have been promised: either punishment, or the Hour.’ Then they will know whose position is worse, and whose host is weaker. (75)
وَيَزِيدُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱهْتَدَوْا۟ هُدًى ۗ وَٱلْبَـٰقِيَـٰتُ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتُ خَيْرٌ عِندَ رَبِّكَ ثَوَابًا وَخَيْرٌ مَّرَدًّا (٧٦)
Allah enhances in guidance those who are [rightly] guided, and lasting righteous deeds are better with your Lord in reward, and better at the return [to Allah]. (76)
أَفَرَءَيْتَ ٱلَّذِى كَفَرَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا وَقَالَ لَأُوتَيَنَّ مَالًا وَوَلَدًا (٧٧)
Have you not regarded him who defies Our signs, and says, ‘I will surely be given wealth and children’? (77)
أَطَّلَعَ ٱلْغَيْبَ أَمِ ٱتَّخَذَ عِندَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ عَهْدًا (٧٨)
Has he come to know the Unseen, or taken a promise from the All-beneficent? (78)
كَلَّا ۚ سَنَكْتُبُ مَا يَقُولُ وَنَمُدُّ لَهُۥ مِنَ ٱلْعَذَابِ مَدًّا (٧٩)
No indeed! We will write down what he says, and We will prolong his punishment endlessly. (79)
وَنَرِثُهُۥ مَا يَقُولُ وَيَأْتِينَا فَرْدًا (٨٠)
We shall take over from him what he talks about, and he will come to Us alone. (80)

(Commentary)

This is the second section concerning their statements reported about them. It constitutes their rejection of the prophetic call on the grounds that it provides no benefit for the well-being of those who believe in it. They argued that even if it were true, it would have brought them the splendor of the life of this world (زهرة الحياة الدنيا), which contains the happiness of living—lofty buildings, precious goods, beauty, and adornment. Thus, they claimed that the state of disbelief they adhere to, which has brought them the good of this world, is better than the state of the believers, who are shrouded in a shabby condition, lack of wealth, and hardship of life. Consequently, they asserted that their disbelief is the truth that ought to be preferred over the faith held by the believers. Allah has responded to their claim with His saying: وَكَمْ أَهْلَكْنا (And how many a generation We have destroyed...) etc., and His saying: قُلْ مَنْ كانَ فِي الضَّلالَةِ (Say, ‘Whoever is in error...’) etc., then followed that by explaining the state of some who were deceived by their words.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا تُتْلى عَلَيْهِمْ آياتُنا (When Our manifest signs are recited to them...) to the end of the verse. The term "station" (المقام) is a noun of place derived from "standing" (القيام), meaning the dwelling or residence. The "assembly" (الندي) refers to a gathering place, and it is said to specifically mean a council for consultation. The meaning of قالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا (the faithless say to the faithful) is that they addressed them directly; thus, the preposition lām is for communication (تبليغ), as has been suggested. Others say it denotes causality (تعليل), meaning they said it because of the believers—that is, for the purpose of misguiding them and turning them away from faith. The first view is more suitable to the context, just as it is more appropriate for the pronoun in "to them" (عليهم) to refer to people in general—including both disbelievers and believers—rather than the disbelievers alone, so that His saying: قالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا (the faithless say) does not involve placing a manifest noun where a pronoun should be.

His saying: أَيُّ الْفَرِيقَيْنِ خَيْرٌ مَقاماً وَأَحْسَنُ نَدِيًّا (Which of the two groups is better in station and more excellent in assembly?) is an interrogative. The "two groups" (الفريقان) are the disbelievers and the believers. Their intention was that the disbelievers are better in station and more excellent in assembly than the believers, who were mostly slaves and the poor. However, they presented it in the form of a question and used metonymy for the two groups, claiming that the believers are so aware of this fact that they would answer accordingly without hesitation or doubt if asked.

The meaning is: When Our signs—which are manifest in their proof and clear in their indication, leaving no doubt for the doubter—are recited to the people (who are the two groups: the disbelievers and the believers), one group among them, namely those who disbelieve, says to the other group, namely those who believe: "Which of these two groups is better in terms of residence and more excellent in terms of assembly?"—and inevitably, they mean the disbelievers. They intend that the necessary consequence of this is that they are the ones who are successful in their way and creed, since they believe there is no happiness beyond enjoying the goods of the life of this world; thus, the truth is what they adhere to.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكَمْ أَهْلَكْنا قَبْلَهُمْ مِنْ قَرْنٍ هُمْ أَحْسَنُ أَثاثاً وَرِءْياً (How many a generation We have destroyed before them, who were superior in furnishings and appearance!) The "generation" (القرن) refers to people associated together in a single period of time. "Furnishings" (الأثاث) refers to household goods; it is said that this term is only applied to a large quantity and has no singular form from its own root. "Appearance" (الرئي) — with a kasrah followed by a sukūn — refers to what is seen of sights and vistas. It is narrated in Majma‘ al-Bayān from some scholars that it is a noun for what is manifest and is not an infinitive (مصدر); the infinitive is rather "opinion" (الرأي) or "vision" (الرؤية). This is indicated by His saying: يَرَوْنَهُمْ مِثْلَيْهِمْ رأي العين (seeing them with their own eyes to be twice as many). Thus, ra’y is the act, and ri’y is the object seen, similar to ṭaḥn (the act of grinding) and ṭiḥn (the ground flour), saqy (the act of watering) and siqy (the water provided), or ramy (the act of throwing) and rimy (the object thrown). End quote.

When the disbelievers argued against the believers regarding the truth of their creed and the invalidity of the prophetic call—which the believers had accepted—by claiming they were better in station and more excellent in assembly in this world, they overlooked that for man there is an eternal, everlasting life (حياة خالدة أبدية) that has no end. His happiness lies only in the happiness of that life, and the few days he lives in this world are of no value compared to that which is infinite, nor do they avail him anything.

Furthermore, these worldly enjoyments do not guarantee him happiness, nor do they protect him from the wrath of Allah should it befall him one day—and it is not far from the wrongdoers. Thus, they are not secure from the displeasure of Allah, nor is there any goodness in a life threatened by destruction, or in a blessing that is exposed to vengeance and disappointment.

He pointed to the answer to this with His saying: وَكَمْ أَهْلَكْنا قَبْلَهُمْ (And how many We destroyed before them). It appears that the sentence is circumstantial (حالية) and the word "how many" (كم) is informative (خبرية), not interrogative. The meaning is: they utter this weak sophistry—"we are better than you in station and more excellent in assembly"—to belittle the believers, while the fact is that We have destroyed many generations before them who were superior in terms of goods and appearances.

The Glorified has narrated a similar sophistry from Pharaoh and followed it with the account of his drowning and destruction. He said: وَنادى فِرْعَوْنُ فِي قَوْمِهِ قالَ يا قَوْمِ أَلَيْسَ لِي مُلْكُ مِصْرَ وَهذِهِ الْأَنْهارُ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِي أَفَلا تُبْصِرُونَ؟ أَمْ أَنَا خَيْرٌ مِنْ هذَا الَّذِي هُوَ مَهِينٌ وَلا يَكادُ يُبِينُ ، فَلَوْ لا أُلْقِيَ عَلَيْهِ أَسْوِرَةٌ مِنْ ذَهَبٍ (Pharaoh proclaimed among his people, saying, ‘O my people! Does not the kingdom of Egypt belong to me and these rivers that flow at my feet? Do you not perceive? Am I not better than this one who is a lowly wretch and can hardly speak clearly? Why have not gold bracelets been cast upon him...’) until He said: فَلَمَّا آسَفُونا انْتَقَمْنا مِنْهُمْ فَأَغْرَقْناهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ فَجَعَلْناهُمْ سَلَفاً وَمَثَلاً لِلْآخِرِينَ (So when they provoked Our wrath, We took vengeance on them and drowned them all. And We made them a precedent and an example for later generations) [Az-Zukhruf: 51-56].

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ مَنْ كانَ فِي الضَّلالَةِ فَلْيَمْدُدْ لَهُ الرَّحْمنُ مَدًّا (Say, ‘Whoever is in error, the All-beneficent shall prolong his respite for him...’) to the end of the verse. The word "was" (كان) in His saying: مَنْ كانَ فِي الضَّلالَةِ (whoever is in error) indicates their persistence in misguidance, not merely the occurrence of some error. By this, the threat of punishing them through "prolonging" (الإمداد) and "gradual leading to ruin" (الاستدراج) — the process of being led further into misguidance as a consequence of initial error — is completed.

His saying: فَلْيَمْدُدْ (let [Him] prolong) is in the form of a third-person imperative, and its meaning resolves to the fact that it is incumbent upon the All-Beneficent to prolong him significantly. For if a speaker commands his addressee to command him with something, its meaning is the speaker making that obligatory upon himself.

"Prolonging" (المد) and "extending" (الإمداد) are the same, but Al-Raghib mentioned in al-Mufradat that "extending" (الإمداد) most often occurs in what is liked, while "prolonging" (المد) occurs in what is disliked. The meaning is that whoever has become settled in misguidance and persists in it—referring to the disbelievers by way of metonymy—Allah has made it incumbent upon Himself to prolong him with that which stems from his misguidance, such as worldly vanities in the context of this discourse. He is thereby distracted from the truth until the command of Allah comes to him, whether by punishment or the Hour, by surprise and suddenness; at that point, the truth will appear to him, but he will not benefit from it.

Thus, His saying: حَتَّى إِذا رَأَوْا ما يُوعَدُونَ إِمَّا الْعَذابَ وَإِمَّا السَّاعَةَ فَسَيَعْلَمُونَ (until when they see what they are promised—either the punishment or the Hour—they will know...) etc., is proof that this "prolonging" is a form of abandonment (خذلان) in the guise of honor. The meaning is that he is turned away from the truth and its following by being occupied with the deceptive splendor of the life of this world; thus, the truth does not appear to him except at a time when he cannot benefit from it, which is the time of the descent of punishment or the onset of the Hour.

As the Exalted said: فَلَمْ يَكُ يَنْفَعُهُمْ إِيمانُهُمْ لَمَّا رَأَوْا بَأْسَنا سُنَّتَ اللهِ الَّتِي قَدْ خَلَتْ فِي عِبادِهِ (But their faith did not avail them when they sighted Our punishment—Allah’s precedent which has passed among His servants) [Al-Mu’min: 85], and He said: يَوْمَ يَأْتِي بَعْضُ آياتِ رَبِّكَ لا يَنْفَعُ نَفْساً إِيمانُها لَمْ تَكُنْ آمَنَتْ مِنْ قَبْلُ أَوْ كَسَبَتْ فِي إِيمانِها خَيْراً (The day when some of your Lord’s signs come, its faith shall not benefit any soul that had not believed beforehand and had not earned some good in its faith) [Al-An‘am: 158].

In returning the plural pronoun in His saying: رَأَوْا ما يُوعَدُونَ (they see what they are promised) to "whoever" (مَنْ), there is a regard for its meaning (معناه), just as in returning the singular pronoun in His saying: فَلْيَمْدُدْ لَهُ (prolong for him) to it, there is a regard for its literal form (لفظه).
