His saying: فَسَيَعْلَمُونَ مَنْ هُوَ شَرٌّ مَكاناً وَأَضْعَفُ جُنْداً (they will know who is worse in station and weaker in hosts) is contrasted with their previous statement: أَيُّ الْفَرِيقَيْنِ خَيْرٌ مَقاماً وَأَحْسَنُ نَدِيًّا (Which of the two groups is better in station and more excellent in assembly?). As for their "station" when they see the punishment—and it appears that the worldly punishment is intended—it is where the punishment of Allah befalls them. The station of the chieftains of the Quraysh to whom the verses were recited, at the time the punishment descended, was the well of Badr into which their bodies were cast. As for their station on the day they see the Hour, it is the eternal Fire, which is the abode of ruin. As for the "weakness of their hosts," it is because they have no protector today from Allah, and everything they prepared for themselves of numbers and equipment will become futile and without effect.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَزِيدُ اللهُ الَّذِينَ اهْتَدَوْا هُدىً (Allah enhances in guidance those who are [rightly] guided...) to the end of the verse. The "lasting righteous deeds" (الباقيات الصالحات) are the righteous acts that remain preserved with Allah and result in beautiful gratitude and a great reward; Allah has promised this in several places in His speech.

"Reward" (الثواب) is the recompense for an action. It is stated in al-Mufradat: The root of "return" (الثوب) is the return of a thing to its original state or to the state estimated and intended by thought—until he said—and thawāb is what returns to a person as a recompense for his deeds; the recompense is called thawāb by conceiving that it is the deed itself—until he said—and thawāb is used for both good and evil, but it is most commonly used for good. End quote. "Return" (المرد) is a noun of place derived from "returning" (الرد), and what is meant by it is Paradise.

The verse is part of the complete explanation of the previous verse. The previous verse explains the state of the people of misguidance and mentions that Allah will prolong them so they wander blindly in their error, turned away from the truth, averse to faith, and playing with the distractions of the life of this world until the punishment or the Hour surprises them and the reality of the matter is revealed to them without them benefiting from it. These are one of the two groups in their saying: أَيُّ الْفَرِيقَيْنِ خَيْرٌ مَقاماً (Which of the two groups is better in station...).

This verse explains the state of the other group, namely the believers, and that Allah, the Glorified, extends guidance to those among them who are guided—the believers—increasing them in guidance upon their guidance. Thus, they are granted success for the lasting righteous deeds, which are better in reward and better in abode—which is Paradise and its everlasting bliss. Therefore, what the believers possess of the goods of life, which is the permanent bliss, is better than what the disbelievers possess of deceptive, vanishing vanities.

In His saying: عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ (with your Lord), there is an indication that the judgment regarding the superiority of the believers' reward and return is a divine judgment that never errs or mistakes.

These two verses—as you see—are a second response to the argument of the disbelievers, namely their saying: أَيُّ الْفَرِيقَيْنِ خَيْرٌ مَقاماً وَأَحْسَنُ نَدِيًّا (Which of the two groups is better in station and more excellent in assembly?).

His saying, the Exalted: أَفَرَأَيْتَ الَّذِي كَفَرَ بِآياتِنا وَقالَ لَأُوتَيَنَّ مالاً وَوَلَداً (Have you seen him who denies Our signs and says, ‘I shall surely be given wealth and children’?) Just as the context of the four preceding verses indicates that the aforementioned false argument was the statement of some polytheists to whom the Qur'an was recited, and who said what they said to refute the word of truth and to misguide and belittle the believers, so too the context of these four verses—which open with a word of wonder, contain a statement similar to the previous one, and conclude with a suitable response—indicates that some people who had believed in the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) or were on the verge of doing so, after hearing the words of the disbelievers, inclined toward them and joined them, saying: "I shall surely be given wealth and children"—meaning in this world—by following the creed of polytheism. They believed there was ill-fortune in faith in Allah and prosperity in taking gods. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, refuted him with His saying: أَطَّلَعَ الْغَيْبَ (Has he peeked into the Unseen...) etc.

As for what the majority have mentioned, based on what has been narrated regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول), that the sentence is the statement of one of those deeply rooted in polytheism from the Quraysh, addressed to Khabbab ibn al-Aratt when he demanded a debt owed to him, and that the meaning of the sentence "I shall surely be given wealth and children" refers to Paradise where he would then pay his debt—this is something that does not fit the context of the verses. For it is well known that the polytheists did not believe in the Resurrection (البعث) at all. Thus, his saying "I shall surely be given wealth and children when I am resurrected and then I will pay my debt" can only be understood as mockery and sarcasm. There is no meaning in refuting mockery with an argument, as is explicit in His saying: أَطَّلَعَ الْغَيْبَ أَمِ اتَّخَذَ عِنْدَ الرَّحْمنِ عَهْداً (Has he peeked into the Unseen, or taken a pledge from the All-beneficent?) etc.

Similar to this statement in its invalidity is what was narrated from Abu Muslim the exegete, that the verse is general for anyone who possesses this attribute.

His saying: أَفَرَأَيْتَ الَّذِي كَفَرَ بِآياتِنا (Have you seen him who denies Our signs) is intended for wonder. The word أَفَرَأَيْتَ (Have you seen) is a term of wonder, and He has branched it with the connective fā’ (فاء التفريع) upon what preceded it of their saying: أَيُّ الْفَرِيقَيْنِ خَيْرٌ مَقاماً وَأَحْسَنُ نَدِيًّا (Which of the two groups is better in station and more excellent in assembly?). This is because the disbelief of this speaker and his saying: لَأُوتَيَنَّ مالاً وَوَلَداً (I shall surely be given wealth and children) is of the same nature as their disbelief and is built upon their statement to the believers that there is no good, happiness of life, worldly honor, or blessing with them, and that there is no good except that which is with the disbelievers and in their creed.

From here it becomes clear that His saying: وَقالَ لَأُوتَيَنَّ مالاً وَوَلَداً (and says, ‘I shall surely be given wealth and children’) has a kind of sequence following His saying: كَفَرَ بِآياتِنا (denies Our signs). He only disbelieved in the signs of Allah assuming that this is a prosperous and blessed path that brings honor and power to its traveler and grants him good and happiness in this world. He has sworn to this, as evidenced by the lām of the oath and the nūn of emphasis in His saying: لَأُوتَيَنَّ (I shall surely be given).

His saying, the Exalted: أَطَّلَعَ الْغَيْبَ أَمِ اتَّخَذَ عِنْدَ الرَّحْمنِ عَهْداً (Has he peeked into the Unseen, or taken a pledge from the All-beneficent?) The Glorified refuted his statement "I shall surely be given wealth and children because of my disbelief" by stating that it is a conjecture about the Unseen (رجم بالغيب) for which he has no way to knowledge. He has not peeked into the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — such that he would know he will be given what he hopes for due to his disbelief, nor has he taken a pledge from Allah such that he could be certain of it. The negation has been presented in the form of a rhetorical question.

His saying, the Exalted: كَلَّا سَنَكْتُبُ ما يَقُولُ وَنَمُدُّ لَهُ مِنَ الْعَذابِ مَدًّا (No indeed! We will write down what he says, and We will prolong the punishment for him significantly.) كَلَّا (No indeed!) is a word of deterrence and rebuke. The end of the verse is proof that the Glorified refutes with it what is implied by this speaker's statement regarding the consequence of being given wealth and children for disbelieving in the signs of Allah. The gist is that what follows from this statement of his is not the giving of wealth and children—for that has other causes—but rather the prolonging of punishment for his disbelief and his conjecture. In reality, by what he says, he is seeking a prolonged punishment where one part follows another, because that is the consequence of his words, not the giving of wealth and children. We will write his statement and arrange its effect upon it, which is the prolonging of punishment. Thus, the verse is similar to His saying: فَلْيَدْعُ نادِيَهُ سَنَدْعُ الزَّبانِيَةَ (Let him call his council! We will call the keepers of hell) [Al-‘Alaq: 17-18].

From here it appears that it is more likely that the meaning of "writing his statement" is its recording so that its effect may follow, not its writing in the record of his deeds to be held accountable for it on the Day of Resurrection, as interpreted by the masters of exegesis. Furthermore, His upcoming saying: وَنَرِثُهُ ما يَقُولُ (And We shall inherit from him what he says) would not be free, according to their view, from a hint of repetition without a clear point.

His saying, the Exalted: وَنَرِثُهُ ما يَقُولُ وَيَأْتِينا فَرْداً (And We shall inherit from him what he says, and he will come to Us alone.) What is meant by "inheriting what he says" is that he will die and perish, leaving behind his statement: "I shall surely be given wealth and children because of my disbelief." This statement was a sin that clung to him as wealth clings to a person, preserved with Allah as if it were wealth inherited after him. In this speech, there is a subtle metaphor (استعارة).

His saying: وَيَأْتِينا فَرْداً (and he will come to Us alone) means by himself, and there will be nothing with him of that which he used to seek help from or rely upon according to his illusion. The gist of the verse is that he will come to Us alone, and there will be nothing with him except his statement which We have preserved for him; thus, We will hold him accountable for what he said and prolong the punishment for him significantly.

This is what is necessitated by building upon the view that his statement at the beginning of the verses, "I shall surely be given wealth and children," refers to being given them in this world. As for building upon the view that it refers to being given them in the Hereafter, as chosen by the majority, the meaning of the verses as they interpreted them is a wonder at the one who disbelieved in Our signs—who was al-‘As ibn Wa’il or al-Walid ibn al-Mughirah—and said: "I swear I shall surely be given wealth and children in Paradise when I am resurrected." Did he know the Unseen such that he knows he is in Paradise?—and it is said: Did he look into the Preserved Tablet?—or has he taken a pledge from the All-Beneficent by saying "There is no god but Allah" such that he enters Paradise by it?—and it is said: Has he offered a righteous deed? No indeed! The matter is not as he said. We will write what he says by commanding the guardian angels to record it in his book of deeds, and We will prolong the punishment for him significantly, and We shall inherit from him what he says—meaning what he has of wealth and children—by Our destroying him and nullifying his ownership, and he will come to Us—meaning he will come to the Hereafter—alone, having nothing of wealth, children, equipment, or numbers.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of the Exalted: أَفَرَأَيْتَ الَّذِي كَفَرَ بِآياتِنا ـ وَقالَ لَأُوتَيَنَّ مالاً وَوَلَداً (Have you seen him who denies Our signs and says, ‘I shall surely be given wealth and children’?)—he said: "It was al-‘As ibn Wa’il ibn Hisham al-Qurashi, then al-Sahmi. He was one of the mockers. Khabbab ibn al-Aratt had a right [a debt] over al-‘As ibn Wa’il, so he came to him to demand it. Al-‘As said to him: 'Do you not claim that in Paradise there is gold, silver, and silk?' He said: 'Yes.' He said: 'Then the appointment between me and you is Paradise. By Allah, I shall surely be given therein better than what I was given in the world.'"

In al-Durr al-Manthur, it is narrated by Ahmad, al-Bukhari, Muslim, Sa‘id ibn Mansur, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, al-Tirmidhi, al-Bayhaqi in al-Dala’il, ibn al-Mundhir, ibn Abi Hatim, ibn Hibban, and ibn Mardawayh from Khabbab ibn al-Aratt, who said: "I was a blacksmith, and al-‘As ibn Wa’il owed me a debt. I came to him to demand it, and he said: 'No, by Allah, I will not pay you until you disbelieve in Muhammad.' I said: 'No, by Allah, I will not disbelieve in Muhammad until you die and then are resurrected.' He said: 'Then when I die and then am resurrected, you shall come to me while I have wealth and children, and I will pay you.' So Allah revealed: أَفَرَأَيْتَ الَّذِي كَفَرَ بِآياتِنا (Have you seen him who denies Our signs...) until His saying: وَيَأْتِينا فَرْداً (and he will come to Us alone)."

I say: Something close to it was also narrated by al-Tabarani from Khabbab. Also from Sa‘id ibn Mansur from al-Hasan from a man among the companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) without naming Khabbab. Also from ibn Abi Hatim and ibn Mardawayh from ibn Abbas from men among the companions.

It has already been mentioned that the narrations do not align with the context of the verses. For the narrations are explicit that the statement was only issued by al-‘As ibn Wa’il by way of mockery and sarcasm, in addition to the fact that definitive reports also support that the polytheists did not believe in the Resurrection and the Gathering at all.

Furthermore, the verses proceed to refute his statement with an argument. If it were a statement of mockery without seriousness, there would be no meaning in arguing against it, as an argument is only valid against a serious statement; otherwise, it would be jesting. Thus, the narrations, despite their explicitness in it being a statement of mockery, do not align with the verse.

Even if it were interpreted in a remote way—that he only said "I shall surely be given wealth and children" by way of forcing an argument (إلزام) and silencing (تبكيت) Khabbab without believing it himself, and not by way of mockery—there would be no reason to mention children along with wealth; it would have sufficed him to say "I shall be given wealth." Moreover, in some of these narrations, he said to Khabbab: "You claim that you will return to wealth and children," yet it was not known among the Muslims of the early Islamic period that there was a common saying that there is procreation and reproduction in Paradise, nor did any part of the Qur'an contain an allusion to that. This is the first point.

Secondly, there would be no reason for the oath and the extreme emphasis in His saying: لَأُوتَيَنَّ (I shall surely be given), as forcing an argument and silencing do not require such emphasis.

Thirdly, there would be no clear point in the absolute use of "being given" (الإيتاء) in His saying: لَأُوتَيَنَّ without restricting it to Paradise or the Hereafter to prevent ambiguity.

Fourthly, nothing would be suitable for refuting and nullifying him except the saying of the Exalted: كَلَّا سَنَكْتُبُ ما يَقُولُ وَنَمُدُّ لَهُ مِنَ الْعَذابِ (No indeed! We will write down what he says, and We will prolong the punishment for him...) until the end of the two verses. As for His saying: أَطَّلَعَ الْغَيْبَ أَمِ اتَّخَذَ عِنْدَ الرَّحْمنِ عَهْداً (Has he peeked into the Unseen, or taken a pledge from the All-beneficent?), it would not apply to him at all. This is because forcing an argument and silencing do not depend on knowing the truth of what one is using to force the argument—such that one would pause at the source of his knowledge—rather, it often coexists with the knowledge of the lie; it only depends on the commitment of the opponent whom one intends to silence by what he commits to or what it necessitates.

Know that regarding the end of His saying: وَالْباقِياتُ الصَّالِحاتُ (and the lasting righteous deeds), there are reports from the Prophet and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them all) which we have alluded to in the thirteenth volume of this book in a tradition-based discussion at the end of verse 46 of Surah al-Kahf.

Ayahs

وَٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ءَالِهَةً لِّيَكُونُوا۟ لَهُمْ عِزًّا (٨١)
They have taken gods besides Allah that they may be a [source of] might to them. (81)
كَلَّا ۚ سَيَكْفُرُونَ بِعِبَادَتِهِمْ وَيَكُونُونَ عَلَيْهِمْ ضِدًّا (٨٢)
No Indeed! Soon they will disown their worship, and they will be their opponents. (82)
أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّآ أَرْسَلْنَا ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينَ عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ تَؤُزُّهُمْ أَزًّا (٨٣)
Have you not regarded that We unleash the devils upon the faithless to urge them vigorously? (83)
فَلَا تَعْجَلْ عَلَيْهِمْ ۖ إِنَّمَا نَعُدُّ لَهُمْ عَدًّا (٨٤)
So do not make haste against them; indeed We are counting for them, a counting [down]. (84)
يَوْمَ نَحْشُرُ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ إِلَى ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ وَفْدًا (٨٥)
The day We shall gather the Godwary toward the All-beneficent, on mounts, (85)
وَنَسُوقُ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ إِلَىٰ جَهَنَّمَ وِرْدًا (٨٦)
and drive the guilty as a thirsty herd towards hell, (86)
لَّا يَمْلِكُونَ ٱلشَّفَـٰعَةَ إِلَّا مَنِ ٱتَّخَذَ عِندَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ عَهْدًا (٨٧)
no one will have the power to intercede [with Allah], except for him who has taken a covenant with the All-beneficent. (87)
وَقَالُوا۟ ٱتَّخَذَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ وَلَدًا (٨٨)
They say, ‘The All-beneficent has taken a son!’ (88)
لَّقَدْ جِئْتُمْ شَيْـًٔا إِدًّا (٨٩)
You have certainly advanced something hideous! (89)
تَكَادُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتُ يَتَفَطَّرْنَ مِنْهُ وَتَنشَقُّ ٱلْأَرْضُ وَتَخِرُّ ٱلْجِبَالُ هَدًّا (٩٠)
The heavens are about to be rent apart at it, the earth to split open, and the mountains to collapse into bits, (90)
أَن دَعَوْا۟ لِلرَّحْمَـٰنِ وَلَدًا (٩١)
that they should ascribe a son to the All-beneficent! (91)
وَمَا يَنۢبَغِى لِلرَّحْمَـٰنِ أَن يَتَّخِذَ وَلَدًا (٩٢)
It does not behoove the All-beneficent to take a son. (92)
إِن كُلُّ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ إِلَّآ ءَاتِى ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ عَبْدًا (٩٣)
There is none in the heavens and the earth but he comes to the All-beneficent as a servant. (93)
لَّقَدْ أَحْصَىٰهُمْ وَعَدَّهُمْ عَدًّا (٩٤)
Certainly He has counted them [all] and numbered them precisely, (94)
وَكُلُّهُمْ ءَاتِيهِ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فَرْدًا (٩٥)
and each of them will come to Him alone on the Day of Resurrection. (95)
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ سَيَجْعَلُ لَهُمُ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ وُدًّا (٩٦)
Indeed those who have faith and do righteous deeds—the All-beneficent will endear them [to His creation]. (96)

(Commentary)

This is the third section concerning what has been narrated about them, namely their polytheism (الشرك) — the act of associating partners with Allah — by taking other deities, and their saying: "The All-Beneficent has taken a son," glorified is He, and the response to that.

His saying, the Exalted: وَاتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ آلِهَةً لِيَكُونُوا لَهُمْ عِزًّا "They have taken gods besides Allah that they may be a [source of] might for them." (19:81). These deities are the angels, the jinn, the saints among humans, and the tyrannical kings, for most of them used to view kingship as possessing a celestial sanctity (قداسة).

The meaning of their being a "might" (عزًّا) for them is that they would be intercessors (شفعاء) for them, bringing them closer to Allah through intercession (الشفاعة) — the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah. Thus, they would attain might in this world, whereby good would be drawn to them and evil would not touch them. Whoever interprets their being a "might" as referring to their intercession in the Hereafter has overlooked the fact that the polytheists do not believe in the Resurrection (البعث).

His saying, the Exalted: كَلَّا سَيَكْفُرُونَ بِعِبادَتِهِمْ وَيَكُونُونَ عَلَيْهِمْ ضِدًّا "No indeed! Soon they will disown their worship and they will be their opponents." (19:82). The "opponent" (الضد) linguistically refers to the incompatible (المنافي) that cannot coexist with a thing. It is narrated from al-Akhfash that the term "opponent" (الضد) is applied to both the singular and the plural, like the terms "messenger" (الرسول) and "enemy" (العدو). Some have denied this, and the justification for using "opponent" in the verse—which is singular—to refer to the "gods"—which are plural—is that since they are united in their enmity toward these people and in disbelieving in their worship, they are treated as a single entity, thus making the singular usage correct.

The apparent context (السياق) suggests that the pronouns in "they will disown" (سَيَكْفُرُونَ) and "they will be" (يَكُونُونَ) refer to the deities, while the pronouns in "their worship" (بِعِبادَتِهِمْ) and "against them" (عَلَيْهِمْ) refer to the polytheists who took those deities. The meaning is: the deities will disown the worship of these polytheists, and the deities will be against the polytheists rather than for them, acting as opponents who harbor enmity toward them. Had they truly been a source of might for them, they would have remained so perpetually. This is supported by His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا رَأَى الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا شُرَكاءَهُمْ قالُوا رَبَّنا هؤُلاءِ شُرَكاؤُنَا الَّذِينَ كُنَّا نَدْعُوا مِنْ دُونِكَ فَأَلْقَوْا إِلَيْهِمُ الْقَوْلَ إِنَّكُمْ لَكاذِبُونَ "When the polytheists see their partners, they will say, ‘Our Lord! These are our partners whom we used to invoke besides You.’ But they will retort at them, ‘You are indeed liars!’" (16:86). Even clearer is His saying: وَالَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ ما يَمْلِكُونَ مِنْ قِطْمِيرٍ ، إِنْ تَدْعُوهُمْ لا يَسْمَعُوا دُعاءَكُمْ وَلَوْ سَمِعُوا مَا اسْتَجابُوا لَكُمْ وَيَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ يَكْفُرُونَ بِشِرْكِكُمْ "And those you invoke besides Him do not control [even] the skin of a date-stone. If you invoke them, they will not hear your invocation, and even if they heard, they would not respond to you. On the Day of Resurrection they will disown your polytheism." (35:13-14).

It is sometimes suggested that the reverse is intended—that the polytheists will disown the worship of the deities and become opponents to them, as in His saying: ثُمَّ لَمْ تَكُنْ فِتْنَتُهُمْ إِلَّا أَنْ قالُوا وَاللهِ رَبِّنا ما كُنَّا مُشْرِكِينَ "Then their only excuse will be to say, ‘By Allah, our Lord, we were not polytheists.’" (6:23). However, this is unlikely because the apparent context indicates that "opponent" (ضِدًّا) is contrasted with "might" (عِزًّا) in the previous verse, serving as a description of the deities rather than the polytheists. Consequently, the deities, who are the "opponent," are the ones disowning the worship of the polytheists, given the specific arrangement of the pronouns.

Furthermore, the appropriate expression for that meaning would be to say: "they will disbelieve in them" (سيكفرون بهم), just as one says: "he disbelieved in Allah" (كفر بالله), and one does not say: "he disbelieved in the worship of Allah" (كفر بعبادة الله).

What is meant by the deities disowning their worship on the Day of Resurrection and being opponents to them is the manifestation of the reality of the matter on that day. For the nature of the Day of Resurrection is the manifestation of realities (ظهور الحقائق) to the gathered assembly, not their origination. If the deities were not disbelievers in their worship in this world and were not opponents to them, but rather this only occurred to them on the Day of Resurrection, the argument of the verse would not be complete; so understand this. Based on this meaning, His saying: "Have you not seen..." (أَلَمْ تَرَ) follows from His saying: "No indeed! Soon they will disown their worship..." etc.

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّا أَرْسَلْنَا الشَّياطِينَ عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ تَؤُزُّهُمْ أَزًّا "Have you not regarded that We have sent the devils against the faithless to incite them [to sin]?" (19:83). Inciting (الأز) and shaking (الهز) carry the same meaning, which is to move something with intensity and agitation. What is meant is the devils' (الشياطين) stirring them toward evil and corruption, and their goading them to follow falsehood and misleading them by shaking them away from firmness and steadfastness (الاستقامة) upon the truth.

There is no harm in attributing the "sending" (إرسال) of the devils to Him, the Exalted, as it is by way of recompense (المجازاة). Since they disbelieved in the truth, Allah recompensed them with an increase in disbelief and misguidance. This is evidenced by His saying: "against the faithless" (عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ). Had it been a primary misguidance, it would have said "against them" (عليهم) without substituting the explicit noun for the pronoun.

The verse, being introduced with "Have you not seen" (أَلَمْ تَرَ)—which conveys the sense of citing evidence—is intended to support what was mentioned in the previous verse regarding their deities being opponents to them. For the devils' stirring them toward evil, corruption, and the pursuit of falsehood constitutes enmity and opposition. The devils, who are among the jinn, are part of their deities. If these deities were not opponents to them, they would not have called them to that which contains their destruction and wretchedness.

Thus, the verse is as if it were said: These deities, whom they consider a source of might for themselves, are actually opponents to them. The confirmation of this is that the devils—who are among their deities—agitate them toward their own wretchedness. Moreover, they are not given free rein; rather, it is by the permission of Allah, which is termed "sending" (إرسال). Accordingly, the verse is connected to the one preceding it, which is evident.

The author of Ruh al-Ma'ani considered this verse to be a consequence of the entire set of verses from His saying: "And man says, ‘What! When I am dead, shall I be brought forth alive?’" (19:66) to His saying: "...and they will be their opponents" (19:82). He elaborated on the nature of this connection in a way that is not beneficial and, in doing so, disrupted the context of the verses and the connection of what follows this verse with what precedes it.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلا تَعْجَلْ عَلَيْهِمْ إِنَّما نَعُدُّ لَهُمْ عَدًّا "So do not be in haste against them; We are only numbering for them a [fixed] number." (19:84). Numbering (العد) is counting (الإحصاء), and counting exhausts the thing counted and brings it to an end. With this consideration, it is intended to mean the exhaustion of their lifespans and the reaching of their final breaths, as if the breaths that extend their lives are stored in their specific number with Allah, and He exhausts them by releasing them one after another until they reach the end, which is the day promised to them.

Since the duration of a human's stay is the period of his trial and examination—as indicated by His saying: إِنَّا جَعَلْنا ما عَلَى الْأَرْضِ زِينَةً لَها لِنَبْلُوَهُمْ أَيُّهُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلاً "Indeed We have made whatever is on the earth an adornment for it that We may test them [to see] which of them is best in conduct" (18:7)—the numbering is, in reality, a numbering of deeds recorded in the scroll of one's life. This is to complete the structure of the eternal afterlife and to fully account for what constitutes a person's life there, whether in terms of blessings or punishments. Just as the fetus's stay in the womb is a period through which its physical creation is completed, so too is the human's stay in this world, so that the creation of his soul (النفس) may be completed and Allah may count and fully deliver what He has decreed for him of provision.

Based on this, a person should not hasten death for a wicked disbeliever, because the duration of his stay is the period for counting his sins so that he may be held accountable and punished for them. Nor should one hasten it for a righteous believer, because the duration of his stay is the period for counting his good deeds so that he may be rewarded and blessed. The verse does not restrict the "numbering," even if at first glance it is understood as the numbering of breaths or days.

In any case, His saying: "So do not be in haste against them" (فَلا تَعْجَلْ عَلَيْهِمْ) is a derivation from what preceded, and His saying: "We are only numbering..." (إِنَّما نَعُدُّ) is the justification for it. It is, in reality, the reason for the delay. The summary of the meaning is: since these people do not benefit from taking deities, and they and their deities are ultimately returning to Us, never leaving Our dominion, nor is their journey on their path without Our permission, then do not hasten against them with seizure or judgment, and do not let your heart be distressed by the delay of that; We are only numbering their breaths or their deeds with a [fixed] number.

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ نَحْشُرُ الْمُتَّقِينَ إِلَى الرَّحْمنِ وَفْداً "The day We shall muster the God-conscious toward the All-Beneficent as a delegation." (19:85). A delegation (الوفد) refers to a group arriving for a visit, to fulfill a need, or something similar. They are not called a "delegation" unless they are mounted (ركبانا), and it is a plural whose singular is wafid (وافد).

It may be inferred from the contrast between His saying in this verse "toward the All-Beneficent" (إِلَى الرَّحْمنِ) and His saying in the following verse "to hell" (إِلى جَهَنَّمَ) that what is meant by mustering them toward the All-Beneficent is mustering them toward Paradise (الجنة). It is called a mustering toward the All-Beneficent because Paradise is the station of His proximity, so mustering toward it is mustering toward Him. The meaning of the verse is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَنَسُوقُ الْمُجْرِمِينَ إِلى جَهَنَّمَ وِرْداً "And drive the guilty to hell as a thirsty herd." (19:86). The term wird (وِرْداً) has been interpreted as "the thirsty." It seems to be derived from "approaching water" (ورود الماء), meaning heading toward it to drink, which only occurs out of thirst. Thus, wird is used as a metaphor for the thirsty. In linking the driving to hell with the attribute of "guilt" (الاجرام), there is an indication of causality (العلية), similar to linking the mustering toward the All-Beneficent in the previous verse with the attribute of "godwariness" (التقوى). The meaning of the verse is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: لا يَمْلِكُونَ الشَّفاعَةَ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّخَذَ عِنْدَ الرَّحْمنِ عَهْداً "They will have no power of intercession, except for him who has taken a covenant with the All-Beneficent." (19:87). This is a second response regarding their taking of deities for intercession: it is not the case that everyone whose intercession a person desires—and thus takes as a god—will be an intercessor. Rather, one only possesses the power of intercession by a covenant (عهد) from Allah, and there is no covenant except for certain individuals among those brought nigh to His presence. He, the Exalted, said: وَلا يَمْلِكُ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ الشَّفاعَةَ إِلَّا مَنْ شَهِدَ بِالْحَقِّ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ "Those whom they invoke besides Him have no power of intercession, except those who bear witness to the truth and they know [it]." (43:86).

It has been said: The meaning is that those for whom intercession is made do not possess intercession except for him who has taken a covenant with the All-Beneficent. The "covenant" (العهد) is faith (الإيمان) in Allah and belief in prophethood. It has also been said: It is His promise to him of intercession, as is the case with the Prophets, the Imams, the believers, and the angels, according to what is in the narrations. It has also been said: It is the testimony that there is no god but Allah, to disassociate from [one's own] power and strength, and to hope in none but Allah. The first view is the most appropriate and is more consistent with the context.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالُوا اتَّخَذَ الرَّحْمنُ وَلَداً "And they say, ‘The All-Beneficent has taken a son.’" (19:88). This is from the saying of the idolaters and some of their elite. Although some spoke of the deities or some of them being "children" of Allah out of honor or veneration, the masses and some of the elite—in the context of teaching—said this as a matter of reality, in the sense of derivation from the essence of divinity (اللاهوت) and the son's inclusion of the substance (جوهرة) of his father. This is what is intended by the verse, and the evidence for it is the use of the term "son" (الولد) rather than "child" (الابن), as well as the argument against its negation in His saying: "There is none in the heavens and the earth..." (19:93) through the end of the three verses.

His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدْ جِئْتُمْ شَيْئاً إِدًّا "You have certainly advanced something hideous!" (19:89) through the end of the three verses. Idd (إِدًّا) with a kasrah on the hamzah means something monstrous and terrible. Tafattur (التفطر) is splitting, khurur (الخرور) is falling, and hadd (الهد) is demolition.

The verses are in the position of magnifying the sin and emphasizing its gravity by representing it through the sensible. It says: By this saying of yours, you have committed a monstrous and terrible thing, from which the heavens are almost rent asunder and split, the earth is almost cleaved, and the mountains almost fall upon the plains in a state of collapse, because they have attributed a son to the All-Beneficent.

His saying, the Exalted: وَما يَنْبَغِي لِلرَّحْمنِ أَنْ يَتَّخِذَ وَلَداً إِنْ كُلُّ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ إِلَّا آتِي الرَّحْمنِ عَبْداً "It does not behoove the All-Beneficent to take a son. There is none in the heavens and the earth but he comes to the All-Beneficent as a servant." (19:92-93) through the end of the four verses. What is meant by everyone coming as a servant (عبدا) to Him is the turning of all toward Him and their standing before Him in the state of pure servanthood (المملوكية المحضة). Each of them is owned by Him, possessing for themselves neither benefit nor harm, nor death, nor life, nor resurrection. This is an actual matter that is inseparable from them as long as they exist. Therefore, the "coming" in the verse is not restricted to the Resurrection, unlike what is in the fourth verse.

What is meant by "He has counted them and numbered them" (أحصاهم وعدهم) is the establishment of servantedhood (العبودية) for them. For servants only have their provisions determined, their duties clarified, and the matters in which they are employed specified after they are counted, numbered, and recorded in the register of servants; through this, servanthood is registered against them.

What is meant by his coming to Him on the Day of Resurrection "alone" (فردا) is his coming on that day empty-handed, possessing nothing of what he appeared to possess in the world, where it was said that he had power, strength, wealth, children, supporters, means, and causes, among other things. It will manifest on that day, when all means are cut off from them, that he is alone, with nothing in his possession, and that he was a servant in the true sense of servanthood (العبودية), never having owned anything and never to own anything. For the nature of the Day of Resurrection is the manifestation of realities.

It becomes clear from the foregoing that what the verses contain of the argument for the negation of a son is a single argument. Its summary is that everyone in the heavens and the earth is a servant of Allah, obedient to Him in his servanthood, possessing nothing of existence or the effects of existence except what Allah has given him. Thus, he receives it while complying with His command and following His will, without owning any of it. And their servanthood is not limited to this; rather, Allah has counted them and numbered them, registering servanthood against them, placing each in his position, and subjecting him to be used in what He desires from him, so he is a witness to his own servanthood. Nor is it limited to this amount; rather, each of them will come to Him alone, possessing nothing and accompanied by nothing. Thereby, the reality of their servanthood to the All will manifest, and they will witness that. If this is the state of everyone in the heavens and the earth, how can it be possible for some of them to be a son of Allah, possessing the reality of divinity and derived from its substance? And how can divinity and poverty (الفقر) coexist?

As for the fact that the existence of things terminates in Him, the Exalted, alone—as contained in the first verse—it is something that those who affirm the Creator (الصانع) do not doubt, whether they are monotheists or polytheists. The disagreement lies only in the multiplicity or unity of the object of worship (المعبود), and the multiplicity or unity of the Lord (الرب) in the sense of the Disposer (المدبر), even if by delegation (التفويض).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ سَيَجْعَلُ لَهُمُ الرَّحْمنُ وُدًّا "Indeed those who have faith and do righteous deeds—the All-Beneficent will endear them [to His creation]." (19:96). Wudd (الود) and mawaddah (المودة) mean love (المحبة). In the verse is a beautiful promise from Him, the Exalted, that He will place love for those who believe and do righteous deeds in the hearts. He did not restrict it to what is between themselves, nor to others, nor to this world, nor to the Hereafter or Paradise. Therefore, there is no reason to restrict it, as some have done, to Paradise, or as others have done, to the hearts of people in this world, and so on.

It has been narrated in the "occasions of revelation" (أسباب النزول) through both Shi'a and Sunni chains that the verse was revealed concerning Ali (peace be upon him). In some Sunni reports, it is said to have been revealed concerning the migrants to Abyssinia, and in others, something else. This will be discussed in the upcoming "Narrative Discourse."

In any case, the generality of the verse's wording remains in place. It appears that the verse is connected to His previous saying: "Soon they will disown their worship and they will be their opponents."

( Narrative Discourse )

In Tafsir al-Qummi, with its chain to Abu Basir, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: كَلَّا سَيَكْفُرُونَ بِعِبادَتِهِمْ وَيَكُونُونَ عَلَيْهِمْ ضِدًّا — "On the Day of Resurrection; that is, those whom they took as deities besides Allah will be opponents on the Day of Resurrection, and they will disassociate themselves from them and from their worship until the Day of Resurrection."

In al-Kafi, with its chain to Abd al-A'la, the client of the family of Sam, who said: "I said to Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): 'The saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: إِنَّما نَعُدُّ لَهُمْ عَدًّا (We are only numbering for them a [fixed] number)?' He said: 'What is it according to you?' I said: 'The numbering of days.' He said: 'Fathers and mothers count those, but rather it is the numbering of breaths.'"

In Nahj al-Balagha, from his (peace be upon him) speech: "A man's breath is his steps toward his appointed term (أجله)."

Also in it, he (peace be upon him) said: "Everything numbered is decreasing, and everything expected is coming."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abd ibn Humayd narrated from Abu Ja'far Muhammad ibn Ali regarding His saying: إِنَّما نَعُدُّ لَهُمْ عَدًّا — He said: "Everything, even the breath."

I say: This is the most comprehensive of the narrations, and it is not unlikely to infer from them that the mention of "breath" in the narrations is by way of example.

In Mahasin al-Barqi, with its chain to Hammad ibn Uthman and others, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: يَوْمَ نَحْشُرُ الْمُتَّقِينَ إِلَى الرَّحْمنِ وَفْداً — He said: "They will be mustered upon noble mounts (النجائب)."

In Tafsir al-Qummi, with its chain to Abdullah ibn Sharik al-Amiri, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Ali (peace be upon him) asked the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) about the interpretation of His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: يَوْمَ نَحْشُرُ الْمُتَّقِينَ إِلَى الرَّحْمنِ وَفْداً. He said: 'O Ali, a delegation (الوفد) only consists of those who are mounted. Those are men who were conscious of Allah (اتقوا الله), the Mighty and Majestic, so He loved them, chose them, and was pleased with their deeds, so Allah named them the God-conscious (متقين).'" — the tradition.

I say: Then al-Qummi narrated another long tradition in which he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) mentions the details of their coming out of their graves, their riding upon she-camels of Paradise, their arrival as a delegation to Paradise, their entry into it, and their enjoyment of the blessings they are provided with.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, from Ibn Mardawayh, from Ali, from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) regarding the verse: "By Allah, they will not be mustered on their feet, nor will they be driven like a herd, but they will be brought she-camels from Paradise, the likes of which the creation has never seen. Their saddles are of gold and their reins are of emerald. They will sit upon them until they knock at the gate of Paradise."

I say: This meaning was also narrated by Ibn Abi al-Dunya, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Ibn Mardawayh through various chains from Ali, from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in a long tradition describing their riding, their arrival, their entry into Paradise, their settling therein, and their enjoyment of its blessings. It was also narrated therein from several compilers of collections from Ali (peace be upon him).

In al-Kafi, with its chain to Abu Basir, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "I said: 'His saying: لا يَمْلِكُونَ الشَّفاعَةَ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّخَذَ عِنْدَ الرَّحْمنِ عَهْداً?' He said: 'Except for him who follows the religion with the guardianship (ولاية) of the Commander of the Faithful and the Imams after him; that is the covenant (العهد) with Allah.'"

I say: It is narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from Ibn Abbas, from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "Whoever brings joy to a believer has brought joy to me, and whoever brings joy to me has taken a covenant with Allah." — the tradition. And it is narrated from Abu Hurayrah from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "Maintaining the covenant is maintaining the five daily prayers." There are other narrations from both the Shi'a and Sunni paths similar to what we have cited. It can be inferred from the sum of them that the "covenant" taken with Allah is a true belief or a righteous deed that saves the believer on the Day of Resurrection, and that what has appeared in the narrations is by way of diverse instances (مصاديق).

Know also that the previous narrations are based on the premise that what is meant by "those who possess the power of intercession" in the verse is those who receive intercession, or both the intercessors and those for whom intercession is made. However, if the intended meaning is only the intercessors, then these reports are extraneous to it.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, with its chain to Abu Basir, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): "I said: 'His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: وَقالُوا اتَّخَذَ الرَّحْمنُ وَلَداً?' He said: 'This was when the Quraysh said that Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, has a son and that the angels are females. So Allah, Blessed and Exalted, said in refutation of them: لَقَدْ جِئْتُمْ شَيْئاً إِدًّا — meaning something great — تَكادُ السَّماواتُ يَتَفَطَّرْنَ مِنْهُ — meaning from what they said and what they attributed to Him — وَتَنْشَقُّ الْأَرْضُ وَتَخِرُّ الْجِبالُ هَدًّا — from what they said and what they attributed to Him — أَنْ دَعَوْا لِلرَّحْمنِ وَلَداً. Then Allah, Blessed and Exalted, said: وَما يَنْبَغِي لِلرَّحْمنِ أَنْ يَتَّخِذَ وَلَداً إِنْ كُلُّ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ إِلَّا آتِي الرَّحْمنِ عَبْداً لَقَدْ أَحْصاهُمْ وَعَدَّهُمْ عَدًّا وَكُلُّهُمْ آتِيهِ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ فَرْداً — one by one.'"

In Tafsir al-Qummi, with its chain to Abu Basir, from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): "I said: 'His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ سَيَجْعَلُ لَهُمُ الرَّحْمنُ وُدًّا?' He said: 'The guardianship (ولاية) of the Commander of the Faithful is the love (الود) that Allah mentioned.'"

I say: It was narrated in al-Kafi with its chain to Abu Basir from him (peace be upon him).

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Mardawayh and al-Daylami narrated from al-Bara, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said to Ali: 'Say: O Allah, grant me a covenant with You, and grant me love with You, and grant me affection in the hearts of the believers.' So Allah revealed: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ سَيَجْعَلُ لَهُمُ الرَّحْمنُ وُدًّا. He said: 'So it was revealed concerning Ali.'"

Also in it, al-Tabarani and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas, who said: "The verse إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ سَيَجْعَلُ لَهُمُ الرَّحْمنُ وُدًّا was revealed concerning Ali ibn Abi Talib. He said: 'Love in the hearts of the believers.'"

In al-Majma', regarding the verse: "It has been said that there are several views: one of them is that it is specifically about Ali; for there is no believer except that in his heart there is love for Ali (peace be upon him)." From Ibn Abbas, and in the Tafsir of Abu Hamzah al-Thumali: "Abu Ja'far al-Baqir (peace be upon him) narrated to me, saying: 'The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said to Ali: Say: O Allah, grant me a covenant with You, and grant me love in the hearts of the believers. He said them, and this verse was revealed.'" A similar report was narrated from Jabir ibn Abdillah.

I say: The author of Ruh al-Ma'ani said: "The apparent view is that the verse, according to this, is Medinan." But you are well aware that there is no indication in any of the traditions that the incident occurred in Medina at all.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf: "When he migrated to Medina, he felt distressed within himself over the separation from his companions in Mecca—among them Shaybah ibn Rabi'ah, Utbah ibn Rabi'ah, and Umayyah ibn Khalaf. So Allah revealed: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ سَيَجْعَلُ لَهُمُ الرَّحْمنُ وُدًّا."

I say: The tradition explicitly states that the verse is Medinan, but this is refuted by the consensus of all that the Surah, with all its verses, is Meccan, as was mentioned at the beginning of the Surah.

Also in it, al-Hakim al-Tirmidhi and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ali, who said: "I asked the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) about His saying: سَيَجْعَلُ لَهُمُ الرَّحْمنُ وُدًّا — 'What is it?' He said: 'Love in the hearts of the believers and the archangels. O Ali, indeed Allah has given the believer three things: affection (المقة), love (المحبة), and sweetness and awe in the hearts of the righteous.'"

I say: al-Miqah (المقة) means love. In this sense are some other narrations from the Sunni paths based on the generality of the verse's wording, which does not contradict the specificity of the occasion of revelation.

Ayahs

فَإِنَّمَا يَسَّرْنَـٰهُ بِلِسَانِكَ لِتُبَشِّرَ بِهِ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ وَتُنذِرَ بِهِۦ قَوْمًا لُّدًّا (٩٧)
Indeed We have made it simple in your language so that you may give good news thereby to the Godwary and warn with it a disputatious lot. (97)
وَكَمْ أَهْلَكْنَا قَبْلَهُم مِّن قَرْنٍ هَلْ تُحِسُّ مِنْهُم مِّنْ أَحَدٍ أَوْ تَسْمَعُ لَهُمْ رِكْزًۢا (٩٨)
How many a generation We have destroyed before them! Can you descry any one of them, or hear from them so much as a murmur? (98)

(Commentary)

These two verses conclude the Surah. In them, the Glorified mentions the descent of the reality of the Quran—which is too sublime to be reached by the hands of ordinary understanding or touched by any but the purified—to the level of remembrance (الذكر) through the tongue of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

He mentions that the purpose of this facilitation (التيسير) is to bring glad tidings to the God-conscious (المتقين) — those who possess godwariness (تقوى), the inner shield of spiritual vigilance against all that displeases Allah — among His servants, and to warn a people who are obstinate (لدا) in their contention. He then summarizes their warning by reminding them of the destruction of the generations that perished before them.

His saying, the Exalted: فَإِنَّما يَسَّرْناهُ بِلِسانِكَ لِتُبَشِّرَ بِهِ الْمُتَّقِينَ وَتُنْذِرَ بِهِ قَوْماً لُدًّا "Indeed We have made it easy in your tongue so that you may give good news thereby to the God-conscious and warn with it a contentious people." (19:97) Facilitation (التيسير) — the act of making something easy and accessible — implies a prior state in which its recitation and comprehension were not easy. The Glorified has informed us of such a state regarding His Book in His saying: وَالْكِتابِ الْمُبِينِ إِنَّا جَعَلْناهُ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ وَإِنَّهُ فِي أُمِّ الْكِتابِ لَدَيْنا لَعَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ "By the Manifest Book! We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason. And indeed it is with Us in the Mother of the Book, sublime and wise." (43:2-4). Thus, He informed that had He kept it in the state it was with Him—and it remains so now—without making it an Arabic, recited text, it could not be expected that people would intellectually grasp it. It would have remained "sublime" (علي) and "wise" (حكيم), meaning it would be resistant and unyielding to the ascent of their understandings or the penetration of their intellects.

From this, it is supported that the meaning of making it easy "in your tongue" is its revelation in the Arabic language, which was his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله tongue. Thus, the verse informs us that He, the Exalted, facilitated it in his tongue so that the task of bringing glad tidings and warning would be made easy for him.

It has been suggested that making it easy "in your tongue" means causing it to flow upon his tongue through revelation (الوحي) and singling him out for the revelation of the Divine Speech so that he may bring glad tidings and warn. While this is a profound perspective in its own right, the first view—in addition to being supported by the aforementioned verses and their like—is more appropriate and consistent with the context of the verses of this Surah.

His saying: وَتُنْذِرَ بِهِ قَوْماً لُدًّا "and warn with it a contentious people" refers to his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله people. The word ludd (اللد) is the plural of aladd (ألد), derived from ladad (اللدد), which means fierce contention (الخصومة).

His saying, the Exalted: وَكَمْ أَهْلَكْنا قَبْلَهُمْ مِنْ قَرْنٍ هَلْ تُحِسُّ مِنْهُمْ مِنْ أَحَدٍ أَوْ تَسْمَعُ لَهُمْ رِكْزاً "How many a generation We have destroyed before them! Can you find any one of them, or hear from them even a murmur?" (19:98) Perception (الإحساس) is cognition through the senses, and rikz (الركز) is a sound; it is said that its linguistic root refers to a faint perception. The sum of the meaning is that although they are argumentative disputants, they cannot frustrate Allah with their contention. For how many a generation (القرن) — a people of a specific era — have We destroyed before them? They perished, such that not one of them can be sensed, nor can even a faint sound be heard from them.
